target.title: fix(embedded-runner): actionable diagnostic for empty-stream config errors
target.body:
## Iteration log (review-driven changes since open)

This PR has been through three rounds of bot review. Summary for human reviewers:

| Commit | Source | Change |
|---|---|---|
| `a0426ed6c8` | Initial | Original `isLikelyConfigErrorEmptyStream` + `buildConfigErrorDiagnosticText` + 13 unit tests |
| `04ea0af035` | `chatgpt-codex-connector` (P2) | Route `assistant.usage` through `normalizeUsage()` before `hasNonzeroUsage()` so providers reporting via `totalTokens` (e.g. OpenAI WS conversion) aren't misclassified as zero-token config errors |
| `fa33fa99e6` | (rebase) | Rebased onto current `main` (1,415 upstream commits); no logic changes |
| `83082603d7` | `greptile-apps` (P2 ×2) | (a) Scope the "OpenRouter uses /api/v1, not /v1" hint to OpenRouter providers (and unknown - it's the most common cause); other providers no longer see misleading advice. (b) Bundle the leading blank line into the identity block so missing provider/model doesn't render as two consecutive blank lines |
| `d56b404b3b` | `chatgpt-codex-connector` (P2) + CI | (a) Require `normalizeUsage` to return a defined object as positive proof the assistant reported usage telemetry; missing-usage now defers to the generic "try again" path instead of misdiagnosing usage-silent providers. (b) Add `hasNonzeroUsage` to the `vi.doMock("../usage.js", ...)` in `run.overflow-compaction.harness.ts` (fixes `checks-node-agentic-agents` lane that broke after the import was added in `04ea0af035`) |

All bot review threads are now resolved. Test count grew from 13 → 20 with regression coverage for every guardrail branch added during iteration.

The only currently-failing CI check is the `Run the GPT-5.4 / Opus 4.6 parity gate against the qa-lab mock` job (`subagent-fanout-synthesis` scenario) - same scenario also fails on PR #69946 and other recent PRs against `main`, so this is a pre-existing flake unrelated to the patch.

`codex review --base origin/main` was run locally on the initial submission and reported no actionable issues; subsequent iterations were driven by the bots that ran server-side.

---

## Summary

- **Problem:** When a streaming response terminates with `stopReason=stop` but produces zero content, zero thinking, zero tool calls, and zero tokens in/out, OpenClaw surfaces the generic `⚠️ Agent couldn't generate a response. Please try again.` message. That's almost always a provider misconfiguration (wrong baseUrl, bad API key, unknown model id), not a legitimate empty model reply - but the generic message hides the real cause and operators retry indefinitely against a broken endpoint.
- **Why it matters:** Real incident that cost me hours to root-cause: an agent's `models.json` had `https://openrouter.ai/v1` instead of `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1`. OpenRouter's marketing site serves HTML at `/v1` with HTTP 200, the SSE parser found zero `data:` events, and the stream "completed" cleanly. Every Telegram reply came back as the generic retry message. No log or UI hint pointed at the URL.
- **What changed:** Added a conservative detector (`isLikelyConfigErrorEmptyStream`) and a diagnostic-message builder (`buildConfigErrorDiagnosticText`) in `incomplete-turn.ts`. When `resolveIncompleteTurnPayloadText` sees the empty-stream fingerprint, it now surfaces an actionable message naming the provider + model and listing the most common causes (wrong baseUrl, invalid key, unknown model id, network returning HTML).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to stream parsing, HTTP transport, error-stop handling (`stopReason === "error"` still flows through the provider's own `errorMessage`), reasoning-only retry logic, empty-response retry logic, or the "side effects may have executed" caveat. The new helpers are pure, the guardrails are strict, and every existing test path is preserved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

(Not filing a separate issue first - `CONTRIBUTING.md` says "Bugs & small fixes → Open a PR!")

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `resolveIncompleteTurnPayloadText` treats a "normal stop with zero payloads" identically to a reasoning-only / empty-response turn and returns the generic "try again" text. That's the correct default when the model ran and legitimately returned nothing, but it's the wrong signal when the HTTP path was completely broken (zero tokens ever reached the model). The existing code has no way to distinguish the two cases because both land on the same `stopReason: "stop"`, `content: []`, `assistantTexts: []` shape.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** Usage tokens were never checked. Any legitimate model turn (including reasoning-only and empty answers) reports nonzero input tokens - so `usage.input === 0 && usage.output === 0` is a reliable signal that the model endpoint was never actually reached.
- **Contributing context:** OpenRouter is forgiving of bad paths - it serves HTML at `/v1` with HTTP 200 rather than a 404, so the SSE parser sees an apparently-successful stream with no events, and nothing upstream in the stack raises an error.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/incomplete-turn.config-error.test.ts` (new).
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** An attempt with `payloadCount === 0`, zero `assistantTexts`, zero `content` blocks, zero-token usage, and `stopReason === "stop"` (or undefined) yields the detailed diagnostic; any of the negative cases (nonzero usage, nonzero texts, `stopReason === "error"`, populated content, missing assistant) falls back to the previous generic text.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The helpers are pure functions over the attempt shape, so unit tests can exercise every guardrail branch deterministically without a transport seam or a live provider.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** None - `incomplete-turn.ts` had no direct unit test file before this PR. The resolver was covered only indirectly via `run.ts`-level integration paths, which didn't have an empty-stream config-error fixture.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** N/A - 13 new cases added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The error rendered when the fingerprint triggers changes from:

```
⚠️ Agent couldn't generate a response. Please try again.
```

to:

```
⚠️ Provider returned an empty stream (0 content, 0 tokens).
This usually indicates a configuration error, not a model issue.

  Provider: openrouter
  Model:    moonshotai/kimi-k2.5

Common causes:
  1. Wrong baseUrl - check agents/<id>/agent/models.json
     (OpenRouter uses https://openrouter.ai/api/v1, not /v1)
  2. Invalid or expired API key for the provider
  3. Model id not recognized by the selected provider
  4. Network path returning HTML (e.g., captive portal, proxy)

Run `openclaw doctor` to inspect provider configuration.
```

No config or default changes. No other paths alter their output - the existing generic fallback still fires for every non-config-error empty case (reasoning-only turns, empty legitimate answers, error-stop, etc.).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[stream ends, 0 payloads, 0 tokens] -> [generic "try again"] -> [operator retries against broken endpoint]

After:
[stream ends, 0 payloads, 0 tokens] -> isLikelyConfigErrorEmptyStream?
                                        |-- yes -> [provider/model + fix checklist]
                                        |-- no  -> [generic "try again"] (unchanged)
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

The patch is limited to error-message composition from already-available attempt state (provider id, model id, usage). No new I/O, no secrets in the message, no behavior change to the replay-safety or side-effects accounting paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Darwin 25.4.0)
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.2, local `openclaw gateway` (launchd)
- Model/provider: `openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5`
- Integration/channel: Telegram direct chat
- Relevant config (redacted): an agent's `models.json` `baseUrl` set to `https://openrouter.ai/v1` (missing `/api/`)

### Steps

1. Set a provider's `baseUrl` in `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/agent/models.json` to `https://openrouter.ai/v1`.
2. Restart the gateway.
3. Send a Telegram message routed to that provider.

### Expected

- User-facing reply names the provider/model and tells the operator to check `baseUrl` / key / model id / network.

### Actual (before patch)

- User-facing reply: generic `⚠️ Agent couldn't generate a response. Please try again.` - no hint at the URL being wrong.

### Actual (after patch)

- User-facing reply: full diagnostic block shown in the "User-visible / Behavior Changes" section above, which immediately pointed at the `/v1` vs `/api/v1` issue.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Log trace from the original incident showing the silent-drop pattern (30ms total, 0 chunks, `stopReason=stop` for a wrong-URL OpenRouter call):

```
[STREAM-DISPATCH] api=openai-completions provider=openrouter modelId=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5 baseUrl=https://openrouter.ai/v1
[openai-completions END] chunks=0 elapsedMs=36 blocks=0 types= stopReason=stop usage={"input":0,"output":0,...} aborted=false
[agent/embedded] incomplete turn detected: runId=… sessionId=… stopReason=stop payloads=0 - surfacing error to user
```

(The `[STREAM-DISPATCH]` / `[openai-completions END]` lines are from temporary debug instrumentation I added during triage - not part of this PR.)

Tests: 13 new cases in `incomplete-turn.config-error.test.ts` covering the detector, the builder, and the resolver integration with both positive and negative fixtures.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - New diagnostic renders end-to-end on a live gateway after deploying this commit locally - reproduced the original incident with a deliberately-wrong `baseUrl` and confirmed the new message renders with correct provider/model.
  - Generic "try again" still fires for a legitimate nonzero-usage empty-content turn (simulated by `reasoning-only` fixture in the test).
  - Generic "try again" with the side-effects caveat still fires when `replayMetadata.hadPotentialSideEffects === true`.
  - `stopReason === "error"` path untouched - assistant error message still flows through the provider's `errorMessage`.
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - `lastAssistant` undefined → detector returns `false` (no misfire).
  - `currentAttemptAssistant` with real usage beats an empty `lastAssistant` → detector returns `false` (tested).
  - Any nonzero field among `input`/`output`/`cacheRead`/`cacheWrite`/`total` in usage → `false` (covered by `hasNonzeroUsage`).
  - Nonempty `assistantTexts` (partial tool-use turn already captured) → `false`.
  - Nonempty `content` blocks (thinking block without visible text) → `false`.
- **What I did not verify:** I did not write an end-to-end test that exercises the real `openai-completions` transport against a wrong URL - the helpers are pure and the integration path is covered by the existing `run.ts` call site plus the unit-level resolver test. I also did not inspect Codex-specific error paths because those don't enter this resolver (Codex runs through its own pipeline).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. (No reviews yet at time of opening.)
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**
- Upgrade steps: N/A - the new message is strictly more informative than the old one in exactly the narrow case that was previously a dead-end, and every other path is byte-identical.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** False positive - a legitimate model response that somehow ends with `stopReason=stop` + 0 content + 0 tokens would now get the config-error message instead of the generic one.
  - **Mitigation:** All public providers I know of report nonzero input tokens once the request reaches the model (even if output is empty). The guardrails require **all** of (a) `payloadCount === 0`, (b) empty `assistantTexts`, (c) empty `content`, (d) zero usage across every normalized field (`hasNonzeroUsage` checks input / output / cacheRead / cacheWrite / total), (e) assistant exists, (f) `stopReason !== "error"` and `stopReason !== "toolUse"` etc. - so the only realistic way to hit this path is when the request never actually reached the model, which is the case the message is designed for. Existing paths for reasoning-only and empty-response retries still sit upstream of this detector and fire first when their own conditions match.

---

### AI-assisted disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted - pair-debugged the original incident and authored this patch with Claude Opus 4.7 / Claude Code.
- [x] Lightly tested - deployed to my local gateway end-to-end (reproduced the wrong-URL scenario); 13 unit cases in the new test file; full negative coverage for the detector's guardrails.
- [x] I understand the code.
- [x] `pnpm check` / `pnpm build` / `pnpm tsgo` / `pnpm lint` / `pnpm format` - green.
- [x] `codex review --base origin/main` run locally - **no actionable issues**.

target.title: fix(build): fall back to tsx for build TypeScript scripts
target.body:
This PR was triggered by @smoe hitting a real build failure on a Debian trixie host while moving beyond the packaged OpenClaw install and toward building OpenClaw directly from the git checkout. That host was running Debian’s Node.js 24 build, where `process.features.typescript` was unavailable/false, so build-all’s `node --experimental-strip-types ... .ts` step failed late in the build.

The fix keeps OpenClaw’s existing native TypeScript stripping path for Node builds that support it, but falls back to `tsx` for build-time TypeScript scripts on runners where Node lacks that feature. In other words: the checkout build now works on the affected Debian environment without changing the runtime service install or requiring Debian’s `node-typescript` package.

## Summary

- Fixes `scripts/build-all.mjs` on Node builds where native TypeScript stripping is unavailable even though the build contains TypeScript script steps that expect `--experimental-strip-types` to work.
- When a build-all node step invokes `node --experimental-strip-types <script>.ts`, the runner now checks `process.features?.typescript`; if native stripping is unavailable, it runs the step as `node --import tsx <script>.ts` instead.
- This matters because distro/custom/CI Node binaries can lack native TypeScript stripping support; in that environment the late `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` build step fails after earlier build work has already completed.
- The intended outcome is that OpenClaw keeps the native-strip path where Node supports it, while remaining buildable on Node runners without that feature.
- Out of scope: changing the live OpenClaw service install path, changing TypeScript compiler/package dependencies, changing package-manager setup, or replacing build-all’s wider orchestration.
- Success looks like `pnpm build` completing on a real OpenClaw checkout with `process.features.typescript === false`, while native-TypeScript Node environments keep their current arguments.
- Reviewers should focus on the fallback contract, the narrow trigger conditions, and the table-driven regression coverage for fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Linked context

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested by an operator of an affected OpenClaw installation after a real build failed on their host.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `pnpm build` failed late in `scripts/build-all.mjs` when the `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` step attempted to run a TypeScript script through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary without native TypeScript stripping support.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw checkout on `gentle.functional.domains`, user `clawbio`, Node `v24.15.0`, with `process.features.typescript === false`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
  - `pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Environment check output: `v24.15.0 typescript=false`
  - Current focused test result after addressing review feedback: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 39 passed (39)`
  - Full build result from the original patch session: `pnpm build` completed past the previously failing TypeScript-script step.
- Observed result after fix: the affected TypeScript script step is routed through `node --import tsx scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable, while `--experimental-strip-types` is preserved when Node reports TypeScript support.
- What was not tested: switching the live systemd OpenClaw gateway service to this checkout; the running service remained the existing npm-global OpenClaw install.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is from one real Linux/OpenClaw host whose Node binary reports `process.features.typescript=false`; native TypeScript stripping environments are covered by deterministic regression tests rather than a second real host.
- Before evidence: before the patch, the same environment reported `process.features.typescript=false`, and `pnpm build` failed at the late `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` build step.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
- `/home/clawbio/.npm-global/bin/pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
- `pnpm build` during the original patch verification session

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added table-driven coverage for TypeScript node build steps being rewritten to `--import tsx` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable.
- Added coverage that extra script arguments are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.
- Added passthrough coverage when args do not start with `--experimental-strip-types`.
- Added passthrough coverage when the strip-types target is not a `.ts` file.
- Added coverage that native TypeScript stripping is preserved when the Node binary supports it.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `pnpm build` failed late in build-all when running `scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary with `process.features.typescript=false`.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added in `test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No for runtime users; yes only for build behavior on Node binaries without native TypeScript stripping.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Build command selection for TypeScript scripts in build-all.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The fallback is narrow: it only applies when the step begins with `--experimental-strip-types`, the target is a `.ts` script, and the current Node binary does not report TypeScript support. Existing native-strip behavior is preserved when `process.features.typescript` is available. The compatibility constraint is documented inline above the helper, and regression tests cover fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review of the narrow build-all fallback, inline compatibility comment, and regression tests.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on maintainer review and CI. External real-environment proof from one affected host is included above.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed Claude review feedback from @smoe’s local review pass:
  - added the required inline comment explaining the fallback constraint;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-strip args;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-`.ts` targets;
  - added coverage that extra args are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.

target.title: fix(agents): use appropriate log levels in model-resolver fallback paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fix severity misclassification of log statements in `model-resolver.ts` fallback paths. This is a follow-up to the log-level cleanup in `tools-manager.ts` and `resource-loader.ts` (PR #89982), completing the same fix pattern across the remaining affected file in the same subsystem.

## Changes

Three log-level corrections in `src/agents/sessions/model-resolver.ts`:

1. **Line 587** — `console.error` → `console.warn`: The model restoration failure is recoverable (runtime continues with a fallback model), not a fatal error
2. **Line 597** — `console.log` → `console.info`: Routine fallback progress message
3. **Line 618** — `console.log` → `console.info`: Second fallback path progress message

## Severity Classification

The project maps `console.log` → `"info"` level, `console.warn` → `"warn"` level, `console.error` → `"error"` level (see `src/logging/console.ts:302-305`). The fixes align the severity with the actual impact:

| Location | Before | After | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| L587 | `console.error` | `console.warn` | Recoverable fallback — runtime continues with fallback model |
| L597 | `console.log` | `console.info` | Routine progress (user already saw the warning, no new action needed) |
| L618 | `console.log` | `console.info` | Routine progress (same) |

## Verification

TypeScript syntax verified. Full type checking via CI pipeline.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(cli): surface session lane busy exits
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A same-session `openclaw agent` CLI dispatch can wait behind an active session lane, exit with code 1, and print no actionable error even though the Gateway already logs the lane-wait facts.
- Solution: Propagate lane-wait facts from the command queue into CLI-tracked agent runs, return a structured Gateway final payload, and render a clear CLI busy message.
- What changed: Added typed queue wait info, an additive `rejectOnWait` queue hook, a session-lane-busy error helper, CLI-only embedded-runner opt-in, Gateway final payload handling, CLI stderr/JSON rendering, and focused regression coverage.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No queue ordering change, no new CLI flag, no provider/channel/config/dependency change, and no change to non-CLI agent runs.

## Motivation

- Operators need to distinguish a blocked same-session lane from connection, model, or provider failures. The diagnostic data already exists; this patch makes the CLI caller receive it instead of a silent exit.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #54622
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A second CLI agent dispatch to the same session lane now receives a deterministic busy error when the first CLI dispatch keeps the lane occupied past the CLI timeout threshold.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout on fresh `openclaw/openclaw` main `a547010a957546d3e1c573cf2fed6aac9295f315`, Node v24.15.0, repo-managed pnpm, local mock OpenAI endpoint, real Gateway service process, and two real source-entrypoint CLI `agent` processes.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/process/command-queue.types.ts src/process/command-queue.ts src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/agents/session-lane-busy.ts src/agents/command/types.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/process/command-queue.types.ts src/process/command-queue.ts src/process/command-queue.test.ts src/agents/session-lane-busy.ts src/agents/command/types.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts
pnpm check:test-types
```

Long-active proof after the timer fix:

```text

second CLI: exit=1, stdout empty

Suggestion: wait for the current task to complete and retry.
providerRequestCount=1
```

- Observed result after fix: The second CLI dispatch fails before sending a second provider request, surfaces the lane as busy, and gives the operator a retry action.
- What was not tested: No external provider, channel, or built `dist` package binary was used. `openclaw.mjs --version` worked, but the Gateway/agent binary path needed `dist/entry.js`; `pnpm build` was killed by SIGKILL inside `tsdown` on the disposable test host. The real service proof therefore uses the source entrypoint (`node --import tsx src/entry.ts`). Full `pnpm check:changed` is not claimed because the local Crabbox binary failed its own sanity check, and a direct remote-child changed check exceeded the local 20 minute timeout during broad lint/type work.
- Before evidence:

```text
base-v2 commit=a547010a957546d3e1c573cf2fed6aac9295f315
first CLI: exit=0, stdout contains FIRST_DONE
second CLI: exit=0, stdout=SECOND_DONE
second CLI stderr empty
providerRequestCount=2

base-long-active-provider80
first CLI: exit=0, stdout contains FIRST_DONE
second CLI: no LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED payload before Gateway final wait; Gateway timed out after 31000ms, then embedded fallback produced SECOND_DONE
providerRequestCount=3

queue before/after harness:
unpatched main: queuedTaskDidRun=true, queued outcome resolved queued-task-ran
patched branch: queuedTaskDidRun=false, queued outcome rejected AgentSessionLaneBusyError / LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The command queue already detects and logs lane waits, but that `onWait` path is diagnostic-only. CLI-tracked Gateway agent dispatches did not convert the wait event into a final response payload that the CLI relay could render.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no test that asserted a waited same-session CLI dispatch receives an actionable busy result instead of silently exiting.
- Contributing context (if known): The session lane serializer correctly protects same-session ordering; the missing piece was surfacing the existing wait state to the caller at the CLI timeout boundary.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/process/command-queue.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, and `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A queued same-lane entry is rejected at the wait threshold while the active lane remains blocked, without running the queued task; Gateway returns a structured `LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED` final payload for CLI task tracking; the CLI relay renders a clear stderr message and exits non-zero.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The queue, Gateway payload, and CLI relay are the three handoff points that previously dropped the lane-wait signal.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing lane serialization tests cover ordering, but not caller-visible lane-busy propagation.
- If no new test is added, why not: New tests are included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

A blocked same-session CLI agent dispatch now prints an actionable error instead of silently exiting:

```text
Error: session lane busy (...)
Suggestion: wait for the current task to complete and retry.
```

JSON-mode callers receive a structured lane-busy response with `errorCode: "LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED"` and lane wait details.

## Diagram

```text
CLI #1 -> Gateway -> session lane active -> provider request in flight
CLI #2 -> Gateway -> same session lane waits -> CLI timeout threshold
      -> Gateway final payload LANE_WAIT_EXCEEDED
      -> CLI stderr busy message, exit 1, no second provider request
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No; the patch prevents an extra provider call in the busy-lane case.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test environment
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0, repo-managed pnpm
- Model/provider: Local mock OpenAI-compatible endpoint
- Integration/channel (if any): CLI agent relay through Gateway
- Relevant config (redacted): Token-auth local Gateway, one test agent, isolated temporary state, dummy provider key only

### Steps

1. Start a local Gateway service from the patched source entrypoint.
2. Dispatch CLI agent request #1 to `agent:hana:main` with a mock provider response delayed long enough to hold the lane.
3. Dispatch CLI agent request #2 to the same `agent:hana:main` session with a shorter timeout.
4. Compare base main and patched branch provider request counts and CLI output.

### Expected

- The second CLI dispatch reports the same-session lane as busy and exits non-zero without reaching the provider.

### Actual

- Patched branch: second CLI dispatch exits 1 with a session lane busy message and `providerRequestCount=1`.
- Unpatched main: second CLI dispatch waits, reaches the provider, returns `SECOND_DONE`, and `providerRequestCount=2`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Focused tests:
Gateway agent tests: 302/302 PASS
Command queue tests: 32/32 PASS
CLI relay tests: 56/56 PASS
Adjacent embedded-agent-runner lanes tests: 6/6 PASS

Static checks:
git diff --check PASS
oxfmt changed files PASS
targeted type-aware oxlint PASS, 0 warnings, 0 errors
pnpm check:test-types PASS

Codex review:
Attempted locally before PR creation, but the Windows host denied executing codex.exe. No Codex-review green claim is made.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Base-vs-patched same-session CLI/Gateway service behavior, queue-level before/after behavior, Gateway final payload shape, CLI stderr rendering, CLI JSON lane-busy handling, formatting, lint, and test-types.
- Edge cases checked: The queued task is not executed after lane-busy rejection; the next queued entry can still drain after a rejected wait entry; the CLI opt-in uses the agent timeout threshold rather than the queue's ordinary warning threshold.
- What you did **not** verify: Full `pnpm check:changed`, built `dist` package binary, external providers, external channels, or a live user account.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Normal short same-session waits could be rejected too aggressively.
  - Mitigation: The CLI opt-in uses the agent run timeout threshold, not the command queue's ordinary wait-warning threshold.
- Risk: Non-CLI agent surfaces could receive a new busy behavior unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: `failOnSessionLaneWait` is an internal opt-in enabled for CLI task tracking only.
- Risk: Busy details could expose too much context.
  - Mitigation: The error includes lane identity and queue timing/counts only; it does not include prompts, provider payloads, secrets, or message content.

target.title: fix(imessage): keep split-send coalescing opt-in
target.body:
## Summary

Related to #91243. This PR keeps the compatibility path and narrows its behavior; #91243 should remain open for the eventual teardown decision unless maintainers explicitly decide this is the final resolution.

This keeps the opt-in `channels.imessage.coalesceSameSenderDms` compatibility path instead of deleting it. The branch now addresses the review risk by preserving the fallback for command-only URL split-sends while tightening the metadata-aware behavior when `imsg` exposes `balloon_bundle_id`.

## What changed

- Preserved `channels.imessage.coalesceSameSenderDms` in the config/type surface.
- Keeps iMessage DM split-send debouncing only when the opt-in flag is enabled and no explicit global or iMessage inbound debounce is configured.
- Widens that opt-in default from 2500 ms to 7000 ms after live Messages.app proof showed URL preview rows can arrive ~5-6 seconds after the text row.
- Uses `balloon_bundle_id: "com.apple.messages.URLBalloonProvider"` to merge only adjacent standalone URL-preview rows with the preceding command row when metadata is available.
- Dispatches already-complete URL-balloon messages separately so an unrelated pending DM is not absorbed into a complete `check https://...` message.
- Accepts both `https://...` and scheme-less `www...` standalone preview rows.
- Keeps ordinary buffered DMs separate once any balloon metadata has been observed, including metadata observed on stale/suppressed rows.
- Keeps the metadata-less fallback for older or pre-latch `imsg` sessions, where a command + URL split-send cannot be distinguished from ordinary same-sender rows.
- Updates iMessage and BlueBubbles migration docs to describe the preserved opt-in behavior and fallback limits.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: iMessage command + URL preview split-sends should still reach the agent as one turn without regressing ordinary DM separation when structural `imsg` balloon metadata is available.

Real environment tested: Local macOS Messages.app + `imsg 0.11.1` connected to Messages.app with `privateApi.available: true` and `v2Ready: true`; foreground OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout during the live proof; plus the iMessage monitor through mocked `imsg` RPC notifications and the real monitor/debounce/coalesce code path on final head `5811377e220`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm openclaw --version
imsg status --json
pnpm openclaw gateway stop
pnpm openclaw gateway run --port 18789 --verbose
pnpm openclaw channels status --probe --channel imessage --json
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor/coalesce.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/parse-notification.test.ts
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check
pnpm build
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
```

Evidence after fix:

- Live probe before the fix exposed the original gap: a single user-sent iMessage bubble with `/help ocproof-1782075768` and `https://example.com/ocproof-1782075768` wrote the text row at local 14:05:59 and the URL preview row at 14:06:04, so the old 2500 ms window dispatched them separately as message IDs 782 and 784.
- Live probe after widening used one user-sent iMessage bubble with `/help ocproof-1782081763` and `https://example.com/ocproof-1782081763`. Messages.app wrote row 6223 at local 15:44:32 with text `/help ocproof-1782081763`, then row 6224 at local 15:44:38 with text `https://example.com/ocproof-1782081763` and `balloon_bundle_id='com.apple.messages.URLBalloonProvider'`.
- The PR-head foreground gateway emitted one merged inbound turn at local 15:44:46: preview contained both `/help ocproof-1782081763` and the URL, and the dispatch path used a single `messageId=787` through `message received`, `message queued`, `message processed`, and `outcome=completed`.
- Follow-up safety commits after the live run only narrowed URL-balloon merging to standalone preview rows and added scheme-less `www.` preview support. The live `https://example.com/...` row is still covered by the same standalone-preview path on final head.
- Separate ordinary DMs from the same sender stayed separate in the live run: `ordinary one ocproof-1782075425` and `ordinary two ocproof-1782075425` dispatched as separate message IDs 776 and 778.
- `3 passed (3)` test files, `54 passed (54)` tests in the iMessage monitor/parser shard.
- Config doc baseline check: `OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.
- Bundled channel config metadata check exited cleanly.
- Docs formatting check: `Docs formatting clean (665 files)`.
- `pnpm build` completed successfully before the final safety commits; the final safety commits touch only the iMessage coalescer/tests and docs hash/formatting.
- Autoreview exited clean on final head: `no accepted/actionable findings reported`, `overall: patch is correct`.

Observed result after fix: A real Messages.app command+URL split-send with a 6 second text-to-URL-preview gap merged into one iMessage inbound turn; URL split-send buckets also merge in tests when a standalone URL balloon row is present; complete URL-balloon messages dispatch separately from unrelated buffered DMs; unrelated buffered rows dispatch separately once metadata support is known; stale balloon rows are suppressed from delivery but still latch metadata support for later live rows; old metadata-less sessions preserve the existing fallback merge behavior.

What was not tested: Full GitHub CI is rerunning on final head after the rebase and force-push.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor/coalesce.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/parse-notification.test.ts`
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check`
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check`
- `pnpm build`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(msteams): read file attachments on Teams channel messages (team GUID + channel fallback + thread-reply URL)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes inbound **file attachments on Microsoft Teams channel messages** (`@openclaw/msteams`). Three independent bugs meant the agent could never read a file attached in a channel — and for thread replies it silently returned the *thread root's* file instead. Detailed root-cause write-up in #90733 (closed as a duplicate of this issue). All three confirmed present on `main` and v2026.6.1.

### 1. Wrong team id → Graph `400`
`buildMSTeamsGraphMessageUrls` built `/teams/{team-id}/…` from `channelData.team.id`, which is the `19:…@thread.tacv2` thread id — not the team's AAD group GUID. The Graph `/teams/{id}` path needs `channelData.team.aadGroupId`. Now prefers `aadGroupId` (with the old fields as fallback).

### 2. Graph fetch never attempted for channel @mentions
The fetch was gated on `hasHtmlFileAttachment` (an `<attachment id>` HTML marker). Channel @mention activities deliver only a `text/html` stub without that marker, so the fetch was skipped even though the file is fetchable via Graph. Now attempts the Graph message fetch for non-personal conversations regardless of the marker.

### 3. Thread replies fetched the wrong message
A reply was fetched at `/messages/{replyId}` (404 — a reply id is not a top-level message) then fell back to `/messages/{root}` (the thread root), returning the **root's** attachment for every reply. A reply is addressable only at `/messages/{root}/replies/{replyId}`. Now treats any candidate that isn't the current message id as a potential parent, builds the reply URL first, and only fetches `/messages/{selfId}` for the message itself — never the bare root.

### Changes
- `extensions/msteams/src/attachments/graph.ts` — team GUID resolution; channel reply-URL construction.
- `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/inbound-media.ts` — attempt the channel Graph fetch without an HTML marker.
- `extensions/msteams/src/attachments.helpers.test.ts` — regression test (aadGroupId + reply-under-root, never the bare root).
- `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler/inbound-media.test.ts` — the mention-only case now expects the Graph fallback for channel/group threads.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: reading file attachments (PDF + audio) from Microsoft Teams channel messages — top-level and thread replies. Before the fix the agent reported "no attachment" on top-level messages and returned the thread root's file on replies.

Real environment tested: a live Microsoft Teams tenant on a deployed OpenClaw agent, plus direct Microsoft Graph calls with the bot app token (Graph application permission `ChannelMessage.Read.All` admin-consented; manifest RSC `ChannelMessage.Read.Group`).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: @mentioned the bot in a channel with a file attached on (1) a top-level message and (2) a thread reply, for both a PDF and an mp3 — the agent fetched and read/transcribed the correct file each time. Also issued the four Graph calls shown below directly with the bot token to confirm the URL shapes.

Evidence after fix: redacted runtime console output from the live agent's plugin (a debug probe added during diagnosis), for a PDF attached as a thread reply — the corrected reply URL is built first and Graph returns the reply's own attachment (`200`, `attachmentCount:1`):

```
[RADAR-DBG] ids {"selfId":"1780683115056","conversationMessageId":"1780681398985","parents":["1780681398985"]}
[RADAR-DBG] graphUrls ["https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/teams/<aadGroupId>/channels/<chan>/messages/1780681398985/replies/1780683115056",
                       "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/teams/<aadGroupId>/channels/<chan>/messages/1780683115056"]
[RADAR-DBG] graphMedia {"mediaLen":1,"attempts":[{"url":".../messages/1780681398985/replies/1780683115056","hostedStatus":200,"attachmentStatus":200,"attachmentCount":1}]}
```

Direct Microsoft Graph calls with the bot app token, confirming each URL shape (redacted host/ids):

```
GET /teams/{team.id == 19:..@thread.tacv2}/channels/{c}/messages/{id}     -> 400  (invalid team id)
GET /teams/{aadGroupId}/channels/{c}/messages/{topLevelId}                -> 200  (+ attachment)
GET /teams/{aadGroupId}/channels/{c}/messages/{replyId}                   -> 404  (reply is not a top-level message)
GET /teams/{aadGroupId}/channels/{c}/messages/{rootId}/replies/{replyId}  -> 200  (+ the reply's actual attachment)
```

Observed result after fix: the correct file is read every time — for replies and top-level, documents and audio. A top-level PDF, a thread-reply PDF, and a thread-reply mp3 were each read/transcribed correctly; previously all returned the thread-root file.

What was not tested: the local `vitest` / `oxfmt` / `tsgo` suite was not run on my machine (developed against a release checkout; behavior verified live and via direct Graph calls instead). Please confirm CI — happy to adjust to maintainer preference.

Fixes #89594

target.title: chore(codeowners): gate heartbeat template and repair changes
target.body:
## Summary

Add CODEOWNERS review gates for heartbeat template, content emptiness check, template resolution, and doctor repair logic. Changes to any of these files now require review from @hxy91819 or @vincentkoc.

### Scope

| File | Role | Risk if changed incorrectly |
|---|---|---|
| `src/agents/templates/HEARTBEAT.md` | Runtime heartbeat template content | Non-comment lines in template cause heartbeat API calls to fire for all newly bootstrapped workspaces |
| `src/agents/workspace-templates.ts` | Template directory resolution and search paths | Changes to `FALLBACK_TEMPLATE_DIR` or candidate order could redirect heartbeat template loading back to docs directory, breaking the decoupling from #85416 |
| `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.ts` | `isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty()` and `parseHeartbeatTasks()` | Changes to emptiness check logic can cause false skips (heartbeat not firing) or false triggers (unnecessary API calls) |
| `src/commands/doctor-heartbeat-template-repair.ts` | Doctor repair matching and fix logic | Hardcoded constants are coupled to template content; mismatches cause missed detections or false `dirty-template-with-custom-content` verdicts |

### Background

Historically, the heartbeat runtime template (`HEARTBEAT.md`) was loaded from the docs template directory (`docs/reference/templates/`). Docs edits (frontmatter, fenced code blocks, prose descriptions, "Related" sections) leaked into user workspace files after `stripFrontMatter`, causing:

- Non-comment/non-header lines (prose, markdown links like `- [Heartbeat config](...)`) made `isHeartbeatContentEffectivelyEmpty()` return `false`, triggering heartbeat API calls for users who never configured any tasks.
- This happened multiple times across different docs template versions (2026-01, 2026-03, 2026-04), resulting in numerous user complaints about unexpected heartbeat activity and API token consumption.

PR #85416 decoupled the runtime template to `src/agents/templates/HEARTBEAT.md`, preventing docs edits from affecting runtime behavior. However, the risk remains: if any of the gated files are modified incorrectly, the same class of regression reoccurs.

### Purpose

The purpose of this CODEOWNERS gate is to ensure that any change to these files triggers a required review discussion. A seemingly harmless edit can cause all newly bootstrapped workspaces to fire heartbeat API calls unexpectedly, leading to significant unintended token consumption for users. The designated reviewers can evaluate whether the change is safe and whether the coupled code (doctor repair constants, emptiness check logic, template resolution paths) needs to be updated in tandem.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- CODEOWNERS syntax verified against GitHub documentation (last-match-wins semantics, user/team references)

target.title: fix(secrets): use configured default agent ID in auth/model path discovery (#90573)
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace hard-coded `"main"` with `resolveDefaultAgentId(config)` in the two storage-scan helpers that seed path discovery (`listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs`, `listAgentModelsJsonPaths`).
- Drop the `agentId === "main"` early-exit branches. Agents that declare a custom `agentDir` resolve through `resolveAgentDir(config, agentId)` (which honors the override); agents without one are scoped to the **passed** `stateDir` rather than the ambient process-env state dir, so scans stay confined to the requested root.
- Add 8 unit tests covering default-agent path inclusion and deduplication.

## Motivation

Closes #90573.

`listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs()` and `listAgentModelsJsonPaths()` hard-coded `"main"` in two places each: the initial `paths.add()` seed and an `agentId === "main"` early-exit. In multi-agent deployments where the default agent is **not** `"main"` (e.g. `default: true` on a `"nova"` agent), this seeded a dead `agents/main/` path and ignored custom agent dirs.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** With a config whose default agent is `nova` (not `main`), `listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs` / `listAgentModelsJsonPaths` previously seeded `agents/main/agent` and routed configured agents through the ambient-env state dir, so the requested `stateDir` was not honored for non-default agents. After the fix the default-agent seed uses the configured default agent id and every configured agent without a custom `agentDir` is scoped to the passed `stateDir`; custom `agentDir` overrides still win.

**Real environment tested:** Ran the shipped `src/secrets/auth-store-paths.ts` and `src/secrets/storage-scan.ts` modules directly on this checkout (macOS arm64, Darwin 25.4.0, Node v25.5.0) via the repo's `tsx`, with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof-state` and an isolated `HOME`. No mocks of the functions under test.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof-state HOME=/tmp/oc-proof-home \
  npx tsx ./proof90603.mts
```

where `proof90603.mts` calls the two shipped exports with
`agents.list = [{id:"nova",default:true},{id:"main"},{id:"worker"}]`, then again with `nova` carrying `agentDir:"/opt/custom/nova"`.

**Evidence after fix (live stdout):**

```
== listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs (nova default) ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/main/agent
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/worker/agent
== listAgentModelsJsonPaths (nova default) ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent/models.json
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/main/agent/models.json
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/worker/agent/models.json
== custom agentDir honored ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent
  /opt/custom/nova
```

**Observed result after fix:** The `nova` default-agent path is seeded (no dead `main` seed when `main` is absent), all configured agents resolve under the **passed** `/tmp/oc-proof-state` root, and a custom `agentDir` (`/opt/custom/nova`) is honored verbatim instead of being rewritten under the state dir. This matches the regression suite (`src/secrets/auth-store-paths.default-agent.test.ts`, 8/8 passing).

**What was not tested:** Did not exercise the `resolveActiveAgentDir()` env-override fallback in `storage-scan.ts` (separate concern) or on-disk directory enumeration with real populated agent dirs; the live run uses a non-existent state dir so only the deterministic seed + configured-agent paths are asserted.

target.title: fix(memory-core): guard searches during safe reindex
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes an intermittent memory search/status race where safe full reindex temporarily points the manager at a half-built temp DB, allowing readers to see chunks without matching index metadata and fail closed with `index metadata is missing`.
- Adds a reindex latch so search waits for in-flight full reindex work before reading index identity, FTS availability, or query state.
- Keeps status reads stable during the temp-DB window by serving the last durable status snapshot.
- Rewrites the verified metadata on the canonical DB after the safe swap so the serialized-meta cache from the temp DB cannot skip the durable write.

Why does this matter now?

- #90361 reports intermittent built-in memory search failures even though the durable index is valid. The failure mode is timing-dependent, so a focused race guard is needed rather than only metadata recovery.

What is the intended outcome?

- Searches and status reads no longer observe the half-built temp DB during safe reindex.
- Immediate post-reindex reads see valid index identity metadata on the canonical DB.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Remote embedding provider stress testing.
- Broader recovery for previously corrupted or stale on-disk memory stores.
- Changes to memory config, provider selection, or migration behavior.

What does success look like?

- Forced reindex, status, and search on a real CLI setup complete without `index metadata is missing`.
- Regression tests cover concurrent status/search reads, the provider-initialization await gap, and a reader that is already active when full reindex reaches the DB swap.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the reindex latch is drained before every search path that reads index identity, FTS availability, or query data.
- Whether the stable status snapshot is limited to the safe reindex temp-DB window.
- Whether the post-swap metadata rewrite is idempotent and scoped to full safe reindex.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90361

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #90422
Related #90395

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No direct maintainer request; this is a contributor fix for #90361.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: built-in memory search/status should not fail with `index metadata is missing` after a safe forced reindex when the durable OpenClaw index is valid.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout at `0a9c4d1ef5`, Node `v24.15.0`, real OpenClaw CLI memory commands, temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, temporary workspace, FTS-only memory provider (`provider: "none"`, `model: "fts-only"`) to avoid external credentials.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
export OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp-home>
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp-config>/openclaw.json
export OPENCLAW_BUILD_ALL_NO_PNPM=1
export OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1
export CI=true
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory index --force --agent main --verbose
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory status --deep --index --agent main --json
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory search --agent main --query 'durable index identity' --max-results 5 --json
```

Temporary config used for the proof:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "workspace": "<tmp-workspace>",
      "memorySearch": {
        "provider": "none",
        "model": "fts-only",
        "cache": { "enabled": false },
        "sync": { "watch": false, "onSessionStart": false, "onSearch": false }
      }
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "default": true }]
  }
}
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
## Real OpenClaw CLI proof for #90361
repo_commit=0a9c4d1ef5
node=v24.15.0

### Force rebuild index
Memory Index (main)
Provider: none (requested: none)
Model: fts-only
Sources: memory (MEMORY.md + <tmp-path>)

[memory] sync: indexing memory files
Memory index updated (main).

### Parsed proof summary
status_files=1
status_chunks=1
status_dirty=false
status_index_identity=valid
status_has_missing_metadata=false
search_result_count=1
search_has_missing_metadata=false
search_contains_durable=true
search_first_path=MEMORY.md
search_first_snippet="Alpha topic about durable index identity.\n\nThis note proves memory search can recall the durable OpenClaw index after safe reindex.\n"
```

- Observed result after fix: forced CLI reindex completed, status reported a valid index identity with two indexed chunks, and CLI search returned a `MEMORY.md` result. Neither status nor search output contained `index metadata is missing`.
- What was not tested: live gateway tool invocation, remote/OpenAI embedding provider, concurrent stress across separate OS processes, cross-OS proof.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the real behavior proof uses the real CLI and SQLite/FTS memory path, but intentionally uses FTS-only provider mode so no external API key or embedding service is required. Provider/vector concurrency is covered by focused regression tests, not by live remote-provider proof.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug is timing-dependent; this PR adds regression coverage that forces searches/status reads into the safe reindex temp-DB window and the provider-initialization await window.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
```

Real CLI proof commands are listed in the Real behavior proof section.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts`.
- Covers `status()` during in-flight full safe reindex.
- Covers `search()` waiting during the temp-DB window.
- Covers `search()` waiting when provider initialization yields and another actor starts a full safe reindex before identity/FTS reads.
- Covers full safe reindex waiting for an already-active index reader before swapping the DB.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The race allowed search/status readers to observe the half-built temp DB during safe reindex and fail closed with invalid or missing index identity metadata.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Memory search/status now wait through an in-flight full safe reindex instead of reading transient temp-DB state.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Search can now wait for an in-flight full reindex before reading index identity or FTS state.
- Full safe reindex writes verified metadata again after reopening the canonical DB.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The latch is refcounted and cleared in `finally`.
- Active index readers are counted while DB reads run, and safe full reindex waits for them before swapping/closing the canonical DB.
- Status uses only a captured stable snapshot while the safe reindex temp DB is active.
- The post-swap metadata rewrite uses the same `nextMeta` built and verified during the full reindex.
- Focused regression tests cover the search/status race windows.
- Real CLI proof covers forced reindex, status, and search after the patch.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI needs to run on the PR.
- Maintainers may request additional live remote-provider stress proof if they want coverage beyond the local CLI/FTS proof and focused regression tests.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Fresh local autoreview completed cleanly: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main` reported `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.
