target.title: docs(telegram): clarify account-local group config
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram docs pointed group setup at root \`channels.telegram.groups\`, which is easy to misread in multi-account configs.
- Why it matters: a bot account can receive a group message from Telegram but still drop it during OpenClaw group policy/auth because the active account has no account-local \`groups\` rule.
- What changed: documented account-local Telegram group rules for multi-account setups and added a non-mutating doctor warning for root groups plus enabled allowlisted accounts missing \`accounts.<id>.groups\`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): runtime authorization and group inheritance behavior are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: A multi-account Telegram setup had a root \`channels.telegram.groups\` rule for a group, but the active bot account lacked \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`. A bot-authored group mention was delivered to the gateway but dropped before agent dispatch.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw gateway with multiple Telegram bot accounts and a private Telegram group used for bot-to-bot coordination. Identifiers are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Verified the local working configuration by copying the group rule into \`channels.telegram.accounts.default.groups["<GROUP_CHAT_ID>"]\`, restarting the installed gateway, and sending a bot-authored \`REQUEST @<bot> ...\` group message from a second trusted bot account.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied redacted runtime log output from the local gateway after the account-local group rule was added:

\`\`\`text
Inbound message telegram:group:<GROUP_CHAT_ID> -> @<BOT_USERNAME> (group, 168 chars)
⇄ res ✓ message.action 310ms channel=telegram conn=<REDACTED> id=<REDACTED>
\`\`\`

- Observed result after fix: Account-local Telegram group config fixed the real setup without a runtime code change. This PR documents that path and adds a doctor warning so similar configs are easier to diagnose.
- What was not tested: I did not deploy this branch as a packaged OpenClaw release. The runtime behavior is unchanged; the new doctor warning is covered by focused tests.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Copied redacted runtime log output from the failing trial before the account-local group rule existed:

\`\`\`text
[telegram][diag] spooled update <UPDATE_ID> completed without agent dispatch kind=message chat=<GROUP_CHAT_ID> chatType=group message=<MESSAGE_ID> from=<TRUSTED_BOT_USER_ID> fromBot=true fromUsername=<TRUSTED_BOT_USERNAME> text="REQUEST @<BOT_USERNAME> <test-message>" outcome=dropped reason=group-policy-or-auth
\`\`\`

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The docs emphasized root \`channels.telegram.groups\` for group setup, while multi-account users need to reason about the active account's group rules.
- Missing detection / guardrail: \`openclaw doctor\` did not flag a config where root Telegram groups existed but enabled allowlisted accounts had no account-local group rules.
- Contributing context (if known): Existing troubleshooting guidance made Telegram privacy/raw delivery and group policy/auth look similar until a diagnostic identified \`group-policy-or-auth\`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: \`extensions/telegram/src/doctor.test.ts\`
- Scenario the test should lock in: root \`channels.telegram.groups\` exists in a multi-account Telegram config, and an enabled allowlisted account has no \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is a non-mutating doctor warning, so the focused doctor scanner/formatter tests cover the new surface directly.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing Telegram doctor tests covered invalid sender IDs, apiRoot warnings, and first-time group guidance, but not this multi-account root-groups trap.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

\`openclaw doctor\` can now warn about multi-account Telegram configs where root group rules exist but enabled allowlisted accounts have no account-local group rules. Telegram docs now show the account-local group config path for multi-account setups.

## Diagram (if applicable)

\`\`\`text
Before:
multi-account Telegram config -> root channels.telegram.groups only -> group update can be dropped by account policy/auth

After:
multi-account Telegram config -> channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups -> account has explicit group authorization
\`\`\`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any \`Yes\`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw source checkout for tests; installed OpenClaw gateway for real behavior proof
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

\`\`\`json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      accounts: {
        default: {
          groups: {
            "<GROUP_CHAT_ID>": {
              requireMention: true,
              allowFrom: ["<OWNER_USER_ID>", "<TRUSTED_BOT_USER_ID>"],
            },
          },
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
\`\`\`

### Steps

1. Configure multiple Telegram bot accounts.
2. Put a group rule only under root \`channels.telegram.groups\`.
3. Send a trusted bot-authored group mention to the active bot account.
4. Move/copy the group rule to \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`.
5. Restart the gateway and resend the trusted bot-authored group mention.

### Expected

- Docs make the account-local group path clear for multi-account configs.
- Doctor warns when root groups exist but enabled allowlisted accounts are missing account-local group rules.
- A correctly configured active account receives the trusted group mention as a normal agent turn.

### Actual

- Before the local config fix, the group message was raw-delivered but dropped during group policy/auth.
- After account-local group config, the trusted bot-authored group mention dispatched normally.
- With this PR, focused tests confirm the new doctor warning and skip cases.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local real setup with multi-account Telegram bots; root group rule did not authorize the active account, account-local group rule did.
- Edge cases checked: disabled accounts, \`groupPolicy: "open"\` accounts, mixed open plus allowlisted accounts, single-account configs.
- What you did **not** verify: full packaged release build or live doctor command from an installed package containing this patch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The doctor warning could be noisy for accounts intentionally configured without group allowlists.
  - Mitigation: The warning skips disabled accounts and accounts whose effective \`groupPolicy\` is \`open\` or \`disabled\`, and tests cover those cases.

target.title: fix(agents): skip implicit provider discovery when models.mode is 'replace' [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `src/agents/models-config.plan.ts:60-61` unconditionally `await`s `resolveImplicitProvidersImpl(...)` (the provider-discovery scan) before reading `cfg.models?.mode`. When the user has set `models.mode: "replace"` to opt out of discovery, this still runs the full implicit-resolver — wasted work and (in some setups) 70+s of startup latency.
- **Why it matters:** The documented contract at `src/config/schema.help.ts:924` is that `models.mode = "replace"` uses only the configured providers. Honoring it eliminates the discovery cost for replace-mode users and fixes the timing regression they were filing the issue about.
- **What changed:** Added a 3-line early-return at the top of `resolveProvidersForModelsJsonWithDeps` that short-circuits to `mergeProviders({ implicit: {}, explicit: explicitProviders })` when `cfg.models?.mode === "replace"`. The injected `deps.resolveImplicitProviders` seam used by tests is preserved for merge-mode callers.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `merge`/`undefined` paths are untouched. `planOpenClawModelsJsonWithDeps` (which also reads `cfg.models?.mode` at L156) is unchanged — its existing logic short-circuits cleanly on the empty `implicit` result. No public type changes. No new exports.

Mirrors the sibling short-circuit at `src/flows/model-picker.ts:116-118` (`loadPickerModelCatalog` does the same `if (cfg.models?.mode === "replace") return ...` before its catalog load).

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

(Closest match: agents config plumbing; no checkbox fits cleanly.)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #66957
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `resolveProvidersForModelsJsonWithDeps` ran the implicit provider resolver even when `models.mode === "replace"`, violating the documented contract and adding 70+s of startup latency in replace-mode setups.
- **Real environment tested:** local clone built and tested on macOS arm64 (Node v24.7.0, pnpm 11.1.0, vitest 4.1.6).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
    src/agents/models-config.replace-mode-skip-implicit-discovery.test.ts \
    src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts \
    src/agents/models-config.providers.implicit.discovery-scope.test.ts \
    src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```
   RUN  v4.1.6 /private/tmp/openclaw-triage-20pr/repo

   Test Files  2 passed (2)        # new test file (under 2 project lanes)
        Tests  6 passed (6)         # 3 scenarios × 2 lanes
   Test Files  6 passed (6)         # existing models-config tests
        Tests  46 passed (46)
  ```
  The new test file `models-config.replace-mode-skip-implicit-discovery.test.ts` has 3 scenarios:
  1. `skips implicit discovery when models.mode === 'replace'` — DI spy assertion `not.toHaveBeenCalled`, result contains only explicit providers.
  2. `still resolves implicit when models.mode === 'merge'` — spy called once, result merges both.
  3. `still resolves implicit when models.mode is undefined (defaults to merge)` — spy called once.
  Regression: all 46 existing tests across `models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts`, `models-config.providers.implicit.discovery-scope.test.ts`, and `models-config.merge.test.ts` continue to pass — they use `mode: undefined` or `merge`, so they enter the same code path as before.
- **Observed result after fix:** all 52 tests pass. With `mode: "replace"` and an injected spy, `resolveImplicitProviders` is never called.
- **What was not tested:** I did not benchmark the wall-clock startup latency improvement in a real OpenClaw setup against the reporter's specific provider configuration (the issue body cites ~72s of `pnpm tsx` cold-load time). The new test proves the implicit-resolver call is genuinely skipped; converting that skip into measured-on-host latency win would require a configured agent with provider-discovery providers loaded.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** on unpatched main, the new test 1 fails — `not.toHaveBeenCalled` is violated because `resolveImplicitProvidersImpl` is awaited unconditionally before the mode is read.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** the function reads `explicitProviders` first, then unconditionally awaits the implicit resolver, then merges. There is no mode check until `planOpenClawModelsJsonWithDeps:156` — too late to skip the discovery work.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** documented contract at `src/config/schema.help.ts:924` ("`replace` uses only your configured providers") was not enforced anywhere in `resolveProvidersForModelsJsonWithDeps`. The new test locks the contract via spy assertion.
- **Contributing context (if known):** PR #73557 attempted a similar shape and was closed without merge. The shape used here mirrors the sibling pattern in `src/flows/model-picker.ts:116-118`, which is the canonical in-repo idiom for replace-mode short-circuits.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/agents/models-config.replace-mode-skip-implicit-discovery.test.ts` (new).
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** with `cfg.models.mode === "replace"`, the injected `resolveImplicitProviders` spy must never be called and the result must contain only the explicit-provider entries.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** uses the same DI seam (`deps.resolveImplicitProviders`) the codebase already uses for testing the merge path. No new mocking infrastructure required.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** none — `models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts` and `models-config.providers.implicit.discovery-scope.test.ts` exercise the merge/undefined paths only.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For users with `models.mode: "replace"`:
- Implicit provider discovery no longer runs. This is the documented contract.
- Startup time drops by the cost of the discovery scan (reported as 70+s in some setups; varies by provider count and `pnpm tsx` cold-load behavior).
- The final `models.json` contents are identical to what the merge-mode emitter would produce given zero implicit providers — i.e. the `providers` map is exactly the user's `cfg.models.providers`.

No change for `merge`/`undefined` mode users.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## AI Disclosure

This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude Code, Opus 4.7). The fix shape was selected after a 5-lens design debate (MVD, defensive, pattern-match, performance, DX) — all five lenses converged on the same 3-line early-return mirroring `src/flows/model-picker.ts:116-118`. The chosen variant is the smallest viable diff that honors the documented contract.

target.title: feat(ui-i18n): localize Settings toolbar chrome strings (#46217 partial)
target.body:
## Summary

Phase 1 of #46217: replaces 20 hardcoded English strings in the Settings view toolbar with `t()` calls under a new `settings.*` namespace in `ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts`. Runs `pnpm ui:i18n:sync` to regenerate all 18 locale bundles. `zh-CN` and `zh-TW` ship with native-speaker translations (seeded into the TM so they survive future sync runs); other locales fall through to English fallback per the existing no-provider behavior.

Strings covered (toolbar chrome only):
- Mode toggle: `Form`, `Raw`, plus the two toggle tooltips and `Raw mode unavailable for this snapshot`
- Changes badge: `Unsaved changes` (raw mode), `{count} unsaved change` / `{count} unsaved changes` (form mode, sing/plural — fix applied after @clawsweeper review), `No changes`
- Action buttons: `Open`, `Open config file`, `Open {path}` tooltip, `Clear`, `Apply` / `Applying…`, `Update` / `Updating…`
- Search: placeholder, aria-label, clear-search aria-label
- Accordion: `Quick Settings` header
- `Raw mode disabled (snapshot cannot safely round-trip raw text).` notice

The `Save` / `Saving…` button reuses existing `common.save` / `common.saving` keys (which already carry native translations across all locales — `保存` / `保存中…` in zh-CN, `儲存` / `儲存中…` in zh-TW). The sing/plural pair `unsavedChangeCount` / `unsavedChangesCount` follows the repo's existing pattern (`common.checkpoint` / `common.checkpoints`).

## Out of scope (separate PRs to follow)

Per the @clawsweeper review on #46217, the full localization touches three additional surfaces that intentionally are NOT in this PR:

- `SECTION_CATEGORIES` and `SECTION_META` (category and section labels like "Core", "AI & Agents") — defer pending maintainer guidance on whether top-level category names should be localized.
- `src/config/schema.labels.ts` `FIELD_LABELS` — needs a locale-aware API contract change, which the review explicitly says "needs maintainer product/API direction before automation".
- Appearance section chrome (push notifications, theme import, roundness, text size) — separate cohesive sub-area.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Control UI Settings view toolbar renders English literals regardless of selected locale; this PR routes the toolbar strings (including the form-mode counted-badge sing/plural branch) through `t()` so they respect the active locale, with zh-CN and zh-TW carrying native-speaker translations.

Real environment tested: Local macOS arm64 checkout, node v22.22.3, pnpm 11.1.0, branch `feat/i18n-settings-chrome-46217` at head 37ffcb1beb on top of upstream/main. Vite dev server started with `pnpm ui:dev` (port 5173) and a real Chrome browser session loaded the regenerated `zh-CN` and `zh-TW` locale bundles via the running dev server, including the new form-mode count keys added after the @clawsweeper review.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
pnpm ui:i18n:sync
pnpm ui:i18n:check
pnpm ui:dev
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/i18n/test/translate.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/config.browser.test.ts ui/src/ui/config-form.browser.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/config-form.search.node.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts ui/src/ui/views/config.ts
pnpm tsgo:core
```

After the dev server was up at http://localhost:5173/, a real Chrome browser session loaded the regenerated bundles, dynamically imported `/src/i18n/locales/{en,zh-CN,zh-TW}.ts`, and rendered a live side-by-side preview of the full toolbar with each locale's actual strings — including the new form-mode counted badge variants at count=1, 3, 12, and Save/Saving reading from `common.save` / `common.saving`.

Evidence after fix:

Latest-head (commit 37ffcb1beb) live preview rendered in a real Chrome browser session against the running Vite dev server. Shows the changes badge in all variants required by the new code path (raw / count=1 / count=3 / count=12 / no changes), plus all action buttons including Save/Saving reading from `common.save` / `common.saving`. All three locales side-by-side using the regenerated bundles:

![Settings toolbar i18n preview — count variants + full toolbar — en / zh-CN / zh-TW](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ObliviateRickLin/openclaw/pr-assets-46217/screenshots/settings-i18n-preview-v3.png)

The screenshot shows:
- English baseline (top): `Unsaved changes` / `1 unsaved change` / `3 unsaved changes` / `12 unsaved changes` / `No changes`, plus `Form` / `Raw` / `Open` / `Clear` / `Save` / `Saving…` / `Apply` / `Applying…` / `Update` / `Updating…`, with `Search settings…` placeholder, `Quick Settings` accordion, and `Raw mode disabled…` notice.
- Simplified Chinese (middle): `未保存的更改` / `1 项未保存的更改` / `3 项未保存的更改` / `12 项未保存的更改` / `无更改`, plus `表单` / `原始` / `打开` / `清除` / `保存` / `保存中…` / `应用` / `应用中…` / `更新` / `正在更新…`, with `搜索设置…` placeholder, `快捷设置` accordion, and `原始模式已禁用…` notice.
- Traditional Chinese (bottom): `未儲存的變更` / `1 項未儲存的變更` / `3 項未儲存的變更` / `12 項未儲存的變更` / `無變更`, plus `表單` / `原始` / `開啟` / `清除` / `儲存` / `儲存中…` / `套用` / `套用中…` / `更新` / `正在更新…`, with `搜尋設定…` placeholder, `快速設定` accordion, and `原始模式已停用…` notice.

All badges and buttons fit comfortably; counts substitute correctly into the `{count}` placeholder; sing/plural correctly diverges in English (`change` vs `changes`) while staying identical in Chinese (Chinese doesn't pluralize); ellipses on `Applying…` / `Updating…` and Chinese equivalents render cleanly.

Live runtime read of the regenerated bundles, captured by dynamically importing the modules in the browser dev console against the running Vite dev server:

```
en.settings.unsavedChangeCount       -> "{count} unsaved change"
en.settings.unsavedChangesCount      -> "{count} unsaved changes"
zh_CN.settings.unsavedChangeCount    -> "{count} 项未保存的更改"
zh_CN.settings.unsavedChangesCount   -> "{count} 项未保存的更改"
zh_TW.settings.unsavedChangeCount    -> "{count} 項未儲存的變更"
zh_TW.settings.unsavedChangesCount   -> "{count} 項未儲存的變更"

en.common.save        -> "Save"
en.common.saving      -> "Saving…"
zh_CN.common.save     -> "保存"
zh_CN.common.saving   -> "保存中…"
zh_TW.common.save     -> "儲存"
zh_TW.common.saving   -> "儲存中…"
```

Real `pnpm ui:i18n:check` console output (post-patch, locally on the branch):

```
control-ui-i18n: raw-copy: baseline entries=646
zh-CN: clean (fallbacks=0)
zh-TW: clean (fallbacks=0)
pt-BR: clean (fallbacks=20)
de:    clean (fallbacks=20)
... 16 more locales reporting clean
```

`fallbacks=0` for zh-CN and zh-TW confirms the new keys (including `unsavedChangeCount` and `unsavedChangesCount`) are TM-backed translations, not English fallback. Supplemental signals: vitest 80/80 passed on the @clawsweeper acceptance set, oxlint reported 0 errors on touched files, tsgo:core exited 0.

Observed result after fix: `ui/src/ui/views/config.ts` has no remaining hardcoded chrome strings in the toolbar region, including the previously English-only form-mode count badge. `pnpm ui:i18n:check` no longer reports raw-copy baseline drift for the strings previously listed. `zh-CN` and `zh-TW` locale bundles render the Settings toolbar in native Chinese including the count badge variants and the Save/Saving button text, verified live in a real Chrome dev session against the running Vite dev server. TM entries seeded for both Chinese locales (all 22 new settings.* keys) so the translations persist across future maintainer sync runs.

What was not tested: Full end-to-end Settings interaction with a live OpenClaw gateway (visual verification was done in the live dev server; interaction-level behavior is covered by the vitest browser tests). Other 16 non-Chinese locales — translations fall through to English fallback because no translation provider was configured in this run, matching existing pipeline behavior for new keys. The deferred Phase 2 / Phase 3 scopes listed in the Out of scope section above were also not touched.

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: feat(skills-setup): add skill setup hook plugin
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw issue #80213 asks for skill author-defined setup hooks; this PR implements the behavior as a bundled plugin instead of adding setup execution directly to core.
- Why it matters: skills that need local setup need a controlled, admin-only execution path with path validation, timeout bounds, and scoped environment overlays.
- What changed: added the `skills-setup` plugin with the `skills.setup` gateway RPC, setup metadata parsing, grouped skill directory resolution, env sanitization, and focused tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not track setup completion state, does not auto-run setup on skill install/update, and does not expose non-admin setup execution. The maintainability follow-up is tracked in #81913 for exposing shared skill registry/frontmatter/env sanitizer helpers through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80213
- Related #81913
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: adds an admin-only `skills.setup` gateway RPC for running an installed skill’s declared setup script.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the repo `pnpm` test/check wrappers.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/skills-setup/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix: `extensions/skills-setup/index.test.ts` passed 5 tests; `pnpm check:changed` passed typecheck, lint, guard checks, and runtime import cycle check.
- Observed result after fix: grouped skills resolve correctly, JSON-shaped metadata is parsed, unsafe env overrides are blocked, invalid slugs/path escapes return `INVALID_REQUEST`, and the setup runner receives the expected script path, cwd, env, and bounded timeout.
- What was not tested: live gateway invocation against a running packaged OpenClaw gateway.
- Before evidence: N/A, new plugin surface.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A

This is a feature implementation for #80213. The plugin currently duplicates some skill registry/frontmatter/env-sanitizer behavior because those helpers are not exposed through the plugin SDK yet; #81913 tracks the maintainability improvement to move those shared contracts into `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/skills-setup/index.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: gateway method registration, grouped skill resolution, metadata parsing, setup env construction, timeout clamping, and invalid request classification.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the test exercises the plugin entry through the registered gateway handler while mocking only command execution.
- Existing test that already covers this: N/A, new plugin.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Adds a bundled `skills-setup` plugin that registers an admin-only `skills.setup` gateway RPC. The plugin can run an installed skill’s declared `metadata.openclaw.setup.script` with a default timeout of 120000ms and a maximum of 600000ms.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[installed skill with setup script] -> [no bundled setup RPC]

After:
[operator/admin calls skills.setup] -> [resolve installed skill] -> [validate setup script path] -> [run script with sanitized env]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: setup scripts can execute local commands, so the RPC is registered with `operator.admin`, validates the skill directory and script path with realpath boundary checks, blocks execution-context env overrides like `HOME`, `PATH`, `SKILL_DIR`, `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, and `XDG_DATA_HOME`, and clamps timeout values.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: repo `pnpm` wrappers, Node 22-compatible project tooling
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: plugin gateway RPC
- Relevant config: test config uses `plugins.entries.skills-setup.config.timeoutMs` and `skills.entries.<skillKey>.env`

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test extensions/skills-setup/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`.
2. Run `pnpm check:changed`.
3. Confirm the new plugin package metadata points to `./index.ts`.

### Expected

- The skills-setup tests pass.
- Changed checks pass.
- No runtime import cycles are introduced.

### Actual

- `pnpm test extensions/skills-setup/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: grouped skill lookup, mixed-case slug normalization, JSON-shaped metadata parsing, env overlay sanitization, timeout clamping, setup path escape rejection, invalid slug error mapping.
- Edge cases checked: direct non-skill directory skipped, grouped skill selected, reserved env keys ignored, unsafe setup path returns `INVALID_REQUEST`.
- What you did **not** verify: live packaged gateway invocation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: no migration needed; optional plugin config may set `plugins.entries.skills-setup.config.timeoutMs`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: setup scripts execute local commands.
  - Mitigation: RPC requires `operator.admin`, validates realpaths, confines scripts to the skill directory, sanitizes execution-context env overrides, and enforces timeout bounds.

target.title: chore(docker): bundle channel voice plugin deps
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: default Docker builds pruned optional bundled plugin runtime dependencies unless operators knew to set `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS`.
- Why it matters: Discord, Feishu, and Voice Call are externalized plugin packages but should be ready in the standard Docker image path.
- What changed: default Docker image builds now include bundled plugin ids `discord`, `feishu`, and `voice-call`; `scripts/docker/setup.sh` uses the same default.
- What did NOT change: operators can still pass `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=` to build a slimmer image without optional bundled plugin deps.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Docs
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: default Docker setup/image-build inputs now include the bundled plugin ids `discord feishu voice-call`, while an explicitly empty `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=` remains an opt-out.
- Real environment tested: Linux repo checkout, Node 22.22.0, Docker setup-script command path exercised through the repository Docker setup harness; full container image build is left to the PR image-build check.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `corepack pnpm test src/dockerfile.test.ts src/docker-setup.e2e.test.ts` and `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 Dockerfile scripts/docker/setup.sh docs/install/docker.md src/dockerfile.test.ts src/docker-setup.e2e.test.ts CHANGELOG.md`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied terminal output:

```text

Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

- What was not tested: full Docker image build locally; I am monitoring PR CI for image-build proof.

Default Docker image builds include Discord, Feishu, and Voice Call plugin runtime dependencies. Custom slim builds can opt out with `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=`.

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: feat(telegram): bind spawned subagents to forum topics
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds an explicit `supportsAutomaticThreadBindingSpawn` channel capability so automatic thread-bound session spawn support is not overloaded onto `defaultTopLevelPlacement`.
- Wires Telegram `subagent_spawning`, `subagent_delivery_target`, and `subagent_ended` hooks so `sessions_spawn(thread:true)` for subagents can create and bind a child forum topic while keeping Telegram's normal top-level binding placement as `current`.
- Makes the spawn capability per-kind (`subagent` vs `acp`) so Telegram advertises subagent forum-topic spawn support without incorrectly exposing ACP child-topic spawn.
- Documents the new capability seam and adds Telegram/core regression coverage for schema exposure, runtime capabilities, hook registration, binding, completion routing, cleanup, and boundary invariants.

## Context

Telegram already had forum-topic primitives and session binding infrastructure, but it lacked the subagent lifecycle hook wiring and an explicit capability to say: "explicit subagent thread spawns can create child topics, while ordinary top-level binding should stay in the current conversation." Discord and Matrix naturally expose automatic thread spawns through `defaultTopLevelPlacement: "child"`; Feishu keeps `current` placement and does not advertise child-thread session spawn. Telegram now follows the narrower model: keep normal Telegram `/focus` and top-level binding behavior current, while allowing explicit `sessions_spawn(thread:true)` for subagents to create a child forum topic when the Telegram adapter can do so.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram `sessions_spawn(thread:true, mode:"session")` from a channel-bound CodeClaw triage session creates and binds a real Telegram forum topic for the spawned subagent session, so follow-up work routes to that topic instead of becoming invisible or falling back to the parent topic.
- Real environment tested: Live `codeclaw-triage` Docker container after installing a custom OpenClaw package built from combined proof branch `codeclaw-proof-82023-82275` (https://github.com/efpiva/openclaw/tree/codeclaw-proof-82023-82275) at `OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (db1c53a)`. The container was connected to the real CodeClaw Telegram forum supergroup and used the real CodeClaw triage cron job.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  docker exec --user codeclaw codeclaw-triage     HOME=/home/codeclaw openclaw cron run cc373f35-b483-4c7f-9253-2f6c9c94a595       --wait --wait-timeout 15m --timeout 30000
  ```

- Evidence after fix:

  The triage transcript for run `1d6d77dd-c23b-4b02-9831-e990fb11a7fc` shows the initial PR action for private CodeClaw PR #1223 was spawned with `thread:true` and `mode:"session"`:

  ```json
  {
    "type": "toolCall",
    "name": "sessions_spawn",
    "arguments": {
      "agentId": "main",
      "label": "codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223",
      "mode": "session",
      "context": "isolated",
      "thread": true
    }
  }
  ```

  The tool result accepted the spawn and returned a thread-bound child session:

  ```json
  {
    "toolName": "sessions_spawn",
    "isError": false,
    "details": {
      "status": "accepted",
      "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819",
      "runId": "23538111-3540-4645-a66b-cc0568f96de0",
      "mode": "session",
      "note": "thread-bound session stays active after this task; continue in-thread for follow-ups.",
      "modelApplied": true
    }
  }
  ```

  Live Telegram/OpenClaw logs show the forum topic was created and bound to that child session. The Telegram supergroup id is redacted, but the topic id and session key are unchanged:

  ```text
  2026-05-17T15:07:57.282-07:00 telegram: bound conversation <telegram-supergroup-id>:topic:1167 -> agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819 (idle=24h maxAge=disabled)
  2026-05-17T15:07:57.406-07:00 [telegram] update: {"message":{"message_id":1167,"chat":{"title":"CodeClaw Activity","is_forum":true,"type":"supergroup"},"message_thread_id":1167,"forum_topic_created":{"name":"🤖 codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223"},"is_topic_message":true}}
  2026-05-17T15:07:57.923-07:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run start: runId=23538111-3540-4645-a66b-cc0568f96de0 sessionId=ada5b4d0-65b5-48ff-8c81-76c2ab477ab8 provider=github-copilot model=gpt-5.5 thinking=medium messageChannel=telegram
  2026-05-17T15:07:58.444-07:00 [agent/embedded] [context-diag] pre-prompt: sessionKey=agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819 ... sessionFile=/home/codeclaw/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/ada5b4d0-65b5-48ff-8c81-76c2ab477ab8-topic-1167.jsonl
  ```

  The persisted Telegram binding file contains the same mapping:

  ```json
  {
    "conversationId": "<telegram-supergroup-id>:topic:1167",
    "targetKind": "subagent",
    "targetSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819",
    "agentId": "main",
    "label": "codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223",
    "metadata": {
      "threadName": "🤖 codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223"
    }
  }
  ```

  The CodeClaw poller state also persisted the spawned native session key for that PR:

  ```json
  {
    "source": "pr_reviews",
    "id": "microsoft.ghe.com|bic/lobster#1223",
    "lastKind": "initial",
    "nativeSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819"
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The live Telegram Bot API created forum topic `1167`, OpenClaw bound it to the spawned `main` subagent session, the child session ran with `messageChannel=telegram`, and all child/specialist session transcript files for the spawned review used the `topic-1167` suffix. CodeClaw recorded `nativeSessionKey` for future follow-ups.
- What was not tested: Telegram permission-denied and non-forum failure paths in the live container; those remain covered by local mocked tests. The live proof used one real forum supergroup and one real CodeClaw triage run.

## Supplemental validation


Behavior addressed: Telegram subagent `sessions_spawn(thread:true)` now has an explicit per-kind automatic-spawn capability and lifecycle hooks that bind spawned subagents to child forum topics without enabling Telegram ACP child-topic spawns.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout with mocked Telegram forum topic creation and session binding adapters; no live Telegram Bot API/forum supergroup was used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/telegram/src/subagent-hooks.test.ts \
  extensions/telegram/index.test.ts \
  extensions/telegram/src/thread-bindings.test.ts \
  src/channels/plugins/thread-binding-api.test.ts \
  src/channels/thread-bindings-policy.test.ts \
  src/agents/runtime-capabilities.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts \
  src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts \
  src/plugins/contracts/boundary-invariants.test.ts
pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md
git diff --check
pnpm build
codex review --uncommitted
```

Evidence after fix: Targeted Vitest proof passed with 12 files and 245 tests; docs MDX check passed; whitespace check passed; `pnpm build` completed including plugin SDK export verification; final Codex CLI review reported no actionable correctness issues.

Observed result after fix: Telegram advertises `thread` for subagent session spawn through the new explicit per-kind capability, returns a routable Telegram delivery origin for created forum topics, routes completion to the bound topic, cleans up Telegram subagent bindings, and does not advertise/recommend ACP thread-bound spawns when ACP support is disabled for Telegram.

What was not tested: Live Telegram Bot API behavior in a real forum supergroup with `can_manage_topics`; non-forum group and permission failure behavior were covered only through local unit paths/mocked failures.

target.title: feat(tools): add text-only profile
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds a text-only tool profile that explicitly denies all tools.
- Surfaces text-only in tool catalog/default profile metadata.
- Allows managed screenwatch frames in local media roots.
- Updates plugin trust audit coverage so text-only is treated as restrictive.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Text-only tool profile should resolve to a no-tool policy and should not make allowlisted extension plugin tools reachable under the plugin trust audit.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS 26.5, Node v24.14.0, production TypeScript imports via tsx, temporary OPENCLAW_HOME and OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR with a real installed extension directory at state/extensions/some-plugin.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```console
$ tmp=$(mktemp -d); mkdir -p "$tmp/state/extensions/some-plugin" "$tmp/home"
$ OPENCLAW_HOME="$tmp/home" HOME="$tmp/home" OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$tmp/state" node --import tsx -e 'import { collectPluginsTrustFindings } from "./src/security/audit-plugins-trust.ts"; import { resolveCoreToolProfilePolicy } from "./src/agents/tool-catalog.ts"; const cfg = { plugins: { allow: ["some-plugin"] }, tools: { profile: "text-only" } }; const findings = await collectPluginsTrustFindings({ cfg, stateDir: process.env.OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ textOnlyPolicy: resolveCoreToolProfilePolicy("text-only"), pluginReachabilityFindings: findings.filter((finding) => finding.checkId === "plugins.tools_reachable_permissive_policy").length, allFindingIds: findings.map((finding) => finding.checkId) }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```console
{
  "textOnlyPolicy": {
    "deny": [
      "*"
    ]
  },
  "pluginReachabilityFindings": 0,
  "allFindingIds": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The real runtime profile resolver returns `deny: ["*"]` for text-only, and the production plugin trust audit returns zero `plugins.tools_reachable_permissive_policy` findings for an allowlisted installed extension under that profile.
- What was not tested: None

## Test Plan
- git diff origin/main...HEAD --check
- targeted secret scan over PR diff
- pnpm exec vitest run src/security/audit-plugins-trust.test.ts src/agents/tool-catalog.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts --reporter=verbose
- pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.media.config.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts --reporter=verbose
- pnpm check

target.title: fix(webchat): preserve code block whitespace
target.body:
Fixes #81339

## Summary
- stop trimming normal WebChat send payloads before `chat.send`
- keep slash/local command matching, dedup, and history on the trimmed text while preserving the user-authored chat text on the wire
- preserve queued and steered queued message whitespace for code blocks and ASCII diagrams

## Real behavior proof

Re-implemented and re-proven on current `main` (rebased to head `6da9e274c2`; the prior head was ~690 commits behind and `main` has refactored the Control UI composer pipeline since the original proof).

**Behavior addressed:** WebChat must send fenced code blocks / ASCII diagrams exactly as typed, so leading spaces and trailing newlines survive instead of being stripped by `.trim()` before `chat.send`.

**Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw gateway built from this PR head on macOS. Control UI rebuilt with `pnpm ui:build`; isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`/`OPENCLAW_HOME`; `gateway run --auth token` on `127.0.0.1:18897` with `gateway.controlUi.dangerouslyDisableDeviceAuth=true` so headless Chrome could connect over local HTTP. The served `/chat` Control UI was driven with Playwright (system Chrome, headless) and the outgoing `chat.send` WebSocket frame was captured via CDP.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. `pnpm ui:build`
2. `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$H OPENCLAW_HOME=$H pnpm openclaw gateway run --port 18897 --token <tok> --auth token --force --bind loopback`
3. Playwright: open `http://127.0.0.1:18897/chat#token=<tok>`, type `` ```\n    indented one\n        indented two\n```\n `` (with extra leading spaces and a trailing newline) into the composer, send, capture the `framesent` WebSocket payload whose method is `chat.send`.

**Evidence after fix:** captured `chat.send` frame (verbatim, token/ids redacted):
```json
{"type":"req","method":"chat.send","params":{"sessionKey":"agent:main:main","message":"```\n    indented one\n        indented two\n```\n","deliver":false}}
```
Programmatic assertions on the captured frame: `sentMessageEqualsRaw: true`, `keepsLeadingSpaces: true`, `keepsTrailingNewline: true`, `pageErrors: []`.

**Observed result after fix:** the served Control UI sends the user text byte-for-byte; the 4-space and 8-space code-block indentation and the trailing newline are preserved on the wire. The whitespace-preservation contract is also locked by a regression test asserting the emitted `chat.send` payload keeps leading/trailing whitespace: `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` -> "preserves user-authored whitespace (code blocks / ASCII) in chat.send" (85/85 `app-chat` tests pass; `oxfmt --check` clean; `git diff --check` clean).

**What was not tested:** end-to-end model response rendering (no live model was configured for the proof gateway; the proof targets the client send path, which is where this fix lives). Local browser run used `dangerouslyDisableDeviceAuth` only to bypass the pairing ceremony over local HTTP; production device auth is unchanged.

PR surface: Source +369, Tests +606, Docs +60, Config 0. Total +1035 across 14 files.

Reproducibility: Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue? Not applicable for the feature request itself because current main and v2026.6.9 simply lack SenseAudio TTS, but the review blockers are source-reproducible from the PR head and current CLI contract.

target.title: feat(senseaudio): add SenseAudio TTS provider
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `senseaudio` bundled plugin only has ASR; no TTS.
- Why it matters: completes the round trip in the same plugin; SenseAudio's `/v1/t2a_v2` is solid TTS and pairs with the ASR added in #66943.
- What changed: registers a `speechProvider` in `extensions/senseaudio/`. Synthesizes via `/v1/t2a_v2`, lists system voices via `/v1/get_voice`, transcodes MP3→opus for `voice-note` targets. Errors split into HTTP / `base_resp.status_code` / missing-audio buckets.
- What did NOT change: no core code; ASR untouched; streaming, telephony, voice cloning, text-generated voices out of scope.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related # 66943
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** SenseAudio bundled plugin now registers a `speechProvider` alongside the existing ASR provider. With `SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY` set, `isConfigured` returns true, `listVoices` surfaces the upstream `system_voice` set via `POST /v1/get_voice`, and `synthesize` returns a real MP3 (or transcoded opus on `voice-note` targets).
- **Real environment tested:** Yes (Linux 5.14.0, Node 22.22.3, production `https://api.senseaudio.cn` with a valid `SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY`, commit `cf9d686a66`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  ```bash
  export SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY=...   # redacted
  pnpm openclaw infer tts convert \
    --text "Hello, OpenClaw, this is senseaudio TTS provider" \
    --model senseaudio/senseaudio-tts-1.5-260319 \
    --voice female_0033_b \
    --output ./hello.mp3 \
    --local --json
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** Captured live stdout from running the `openclaw` command above on head commit `cf9d686a66`:

  ```json
  {
    "ok": true,
    "capability": "tts.convert",
    "transport": "local",
    "provider": "senseaudio",
    "attempts": [
      {
        "provider": "senseaudio",
        "outcome": "success",
        "reasonCode": "success",
        "personaBinding": "none",
        "latencyMs": 3595
      }
    ],
    "outputs": [
      {
        "path": "/mnt/cache/haoxin/vc/openclaw/hello.mp3",
        "format": "mp3",
        "voiceCompatible": false
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

  ![terminal screenshot](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d62bae66-0e43-4d3e-b506-b78646934335)

  The produced `./hello.mp3` is a valid MP3 file (ID3 header) that plays back as the input text. Attached audio artifact: [hello.mp3](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27789675/hello.mp3).

- **Observed result after fix:** SenseAudio TTS provider registered and reachable end-to-end via the gateway-bypassing `--local` path; voice-list endpoint reachable; produced MP3 plays back as the input text.

- **What was not tested:**
  - Voice-note opus transcoding end-to-end through a real messaging channel (Telegram / iMessage / WhatsApp).
  - Telephony, streaming, voice cloning, and text-generated voices (intentionally out of scope).
  - Full `pnpm test` / `pnpm test:contracts:channels` suites — only targeted `pnpm test:extension senseaudio` and `pnpm test:contracts:plugins` lanes were executed.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A (feature, not a bug fix).
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — new feature, no regression surface.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/senseaudio/tts.test.ts`, `extensions/senseaudio/speech-provider.test.ts`, `extensions/senseaudio/senseaudio.live.test.ts`, and `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-registration.senseaudio.contract.test.ts` (driven by the new entry in `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-registration-contract-cases.ts`).
- Scenario the test should lock in: registration contract guards the `speechProvider` id and `listVoices` requirement; network-layer tests lock in the `/v1/t2a_v2` body shape, the three error buckets, the hex decode, and the `/v1/get_voice` "only system voices" mapping; adapter-layer tests lock in config / env-var fallback / `isConfigured` / minimal `parseDirectiveToken` / both `synthesize` paths; live test guards the production round trip.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: those are the only surfaces refactors can plausibly break — upstream contract drift, talk-voice wiring, registered-capability loss.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A — tests added by this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: tests are included in this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `senseaudio` appears in `openclaw infer tts providers`, "configured" when `SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY` is in env or `messages.tts.providers.senseaudio.apiKey`.
- `openclaw infer tts voices --provider senseaudio` lists the upstream system voices.
- `openclaw infer tts convert --model senseaudio/senseaudio-tts-1.5-260319 ...` returns MP3 (or opus for voice-note channel targets).
- `autoSelectOrder: 45` — strictly behind ElevenLabs (20), Azure (30), MiniMax (40). Existing user defaults are not displaced.
- Defaults: voice `female_0033_b`, model `senseaudio-tts-1.5-260319`, base URL `https://api.senseaudio.cn`, MP3 32 kHz / 128 kbps / stereo.
- No new env vars / config keys / CLI flags. `SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY` was already declared by the ASR side.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
audio-file target:
  tts.convert -> senseaudio.synthesize
              -> POST /v1/t2a_v2 -> hex audio
              -> Buffer.from(hex) -> MP3 (voiceCompatible=false)

voice-note target:
  tts.convert (channel=voice-note) -> senseaudio.synthesize
              -> POST /v1/t2a_v2 -> MP3
              -> transcodeAudioBufferToOpus (ffmpeg)
              -> opus (voiceCompatible=true) -> messenger voice-note bubble

voice picker:
  talk UI -> senseaudio.listVoices
          -> POST /v1/get_voice {voice_type: "system"}
          -> map system_voice[] -> SpeechVoiceOption[]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` (reuses `SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY`; configured values go through `normalizeResolvedSecretInputString`; key never logged).
- New/changed network calls? `Yes`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Two new outbound HTTPS calls to `api.senseaudio.cn` (`/v1/t2a_v2`, `/v1/get_voice`). Both routed through `fetchWithSsrFGuard` + `ssrfPolicyFromHttpBaseUrlAllowedHostname`. HTTP errors via `assertOkOrThrowProviderError`. Business errors surface `status_msg`, never the request key. `voice-note` transcoding reuses the SDK-managed `transcodeAudioBufferToOpus`. `listVoices` enforces a 15s timeout.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (5.14.0-284.25.1.el9_2.x86_64)
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.3 (nvm); pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: SenseAudio `senseaudio-tts-1.5-260319`
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for the CLI repro
- Relevant config (redacted): `SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY=sk-...` in env; no config-file changes required

### Steps

1. `export SENSEAUDIO_API_KEY=sk-...`
2. `pnpm openclaw infer tts convert --text "Hello, OpenClaw" --model senseaudio/senseaudio-tts-1.5-260319 --voice female_0033_b --output ./hello.mp3 --local --json`
3. Inspect the JSON envelope and play `./hello.mp3`.

### Expected

- Envelope: `provider: "senseaudio"`, `outputs[0].format: "mp3"`, writable `path`.
- `./hello.mp3` is a valid MP3 that plays back the input text.

### Actual

- Envelope exactly as expected.
- `./hello.mp3` plays back as the input.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: live `tts convert` returns a playable MP3; `pnpm test:extension senseaudio` 31/31; three boundary inventories 0 violations; tsgo/oxlint/oxfmt clean; senseaudio plugin-registration contract passes.
- Edge cases checked: `base_resp.status_code != 0` surfaces `status_msg`; missing `data.audio` triggers "no audio data"; `listVoices` drops cloning/generation, only maps `system_voice`; empty `description: []` doesn't leak as empty string; `voice-note` path invokes the transcoder and flips `voiceCompatible: true`; API key resolution falls back from config to env to "missing".
- What you did **not** verify: voice-note opus end-to-end through a real messenger; telephony / streaming / voice cloning / text-generated voices (all intentionally out of scope).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: existing users with another default TTS provider might be silently reassigned to SenseAudio.
  - Mitigation: `autoSelectOrder: 45` — strictly behind every existing TTS provider; mirrors the ASR side's `autoPriority: 40`.
- Risk: voice-note opus transcoding depends on ffmpeg.
  - Mitigation: reuses the same `transcodeAudioBufferToOpus` SDK helper that MiniMax already relies on; no new ffmpeg dependency or failure semantics.
- Risk: a slow upstream could hang the voice-picker dropdown.
  - Mitigation: `listSenseAudioSystemVoices` carries a 15s `SENSEAUDIO_LIST_VOICES_TIMEOUT_MS`.
- Risk: a hostile upstream returning a huge hex payload could allocate a large `Buffer`.
  - Mitigation: SSRF guard restricts the host; can add a hard byte cap in a follow-up if needed.

## AI-assisted

Developed with **Claude Code**. Implementation, tests, and review iterations generated and applied by Claude; the contributor reviewed each step, supplied credentials, and ran the live verification locally.
