target.title: Prune completed Codex Responses tool replay
target.body:
## Summary
- prune completed OpenAI Codex native Responses tool-call replay after a later assistant/user turn has consumed the tool output
- keep active tail function call outputs so tool continuation still works
- add synthetic regression coverage for completed replay pruning and active tail preservation

## Root cause
Native Codex Responses rejects `function_call_output` items when the request is not currently continuing the matching `function_call`. Long-lived session histories can contain already-consumed tool calls and tool results from earlier turns, so replaying those historical outputs can make later turns fail before the model responds.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Long-lived OpenClaw channel and main-agent follow-up turns no longer fail by replaying an already-consumed Codex Responses `function_call_output`.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway with the OpenAI Codex native Responses provider, using the installed runtime patched with the same replay-pruning logic. Account details, paths, channel names, session identifiers, prompts, and concrete call IDs are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw gateway restart`; `openclaw config validate`; `openclaw health`; `openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-channel-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json`; `openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-main-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json`; `rg "No tool call found for function call output" <redacted-gateway-error-log>`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript, redacted:

```text
$ openclaw config validate
Config valid

$ openclaw health
gateway ok; default agent available

$ openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-channel-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json
text: received
provider: openai-codex
model: gpt-5.5
fallbackUsed: false
stopReason: stop

$ openclaw agent --session-id <redacted-main-session> --message <redacted> --thinking low --json
text: received
provider: openai-codex
model: gpt-5.5
fallbackUsed: false
stopReason: stop

$ rg "No tool call found for function call output" <redacted-gateway-error-log>
no new matches during the post-fix smoke checks
```

- Observed result after fix: The previously failing follow-up path returned normal assistant text in both the channel-originated conversation and the main-agent conversation, stayed on `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, did not use fallback routing, and did not add new gateway errors for the rejected `function_call_output` shape during the smoke checks.
- What was not tested: No known runtime gaps for the reproduced channel/main follow-up path. Full upstream CI is left to the repository workflow.

## Checks
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.responses-tool-replay.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts src/agents/openai-transport-stream.responses-tool-replay.test.ts`

## Notes
- Targeted vitest was attempted from a sparse checkout but did not reach the test body because the local install lacked the built `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` artifact from the git dependency. The regression test is included for CI/full-checkout execution.

target.title: docs: clarify backend client behavior with gateway.auth.mode 'none'
target.body:
## Summary

This PR clarifies an important behavior of `gateway.auth.mode: "none"` that is not currently documented.

## Problem

When `gateway.auth.mode` is set to `"none"`, the gateway does not generate or provide a shared authentication token. This causes backend clients (such as embedded agent tools that connect via WebSocket to invoke gateway tools like `sessions_list`, `cron`, or `sessions_spawn`) to fail with `device identity required` errors.

## Evidence

From Gateway logs:

```
# Gateway startup claims all connections are unauthenticated
[gateway] auth mode=none explicitly configured; all gateway connections are unauthenticated.

# But backend clients still fail
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: device identity required
[tools] sessions_list failed: gateway closed (1008): device identity required
```

## Solution

Use `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` instead of `"none"` when backend clients need to connect to the gateway.

## Changes

Added a note to the `gateway.auth.mode` documentation in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` to clarify:

1. When set to `none`, the gateway does not generate a shared token
2. Backend clients will fail with `device identity required` errors
3. Recommend using `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` for backend client compatibility

## Testing

This behavior was observed and verified through actual testing:

| `gateway.auth.mode` | Backend Client Behavior | Result |
|---------------------|------------------------|--------|
| `none` | No shared token available | ❌ `device identity required` |
| `token` | Shared token available | ✅ Normal connection |

## Related

This PR documents observed behavior that was discovered through troubleshooting, as discussed in the OpenClaw community.

target.title: fix(google): use parameters not parametersJsonSchema in realtime FunctionDeclarations
target.body:
## Summary

Switch the Gemini Live realtime voice provider's `FunctionDeclaration` from `parametersJsonSchema` to `parameters` so the preview audio models actually honor tool parameter schemas.

## The bug

The Live API preview audio models (`gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-*`) silently ignore `parametersJsonSchema` — they read only the function `description` and invent parameter names like `query`, `prompt`, `description`, etc. Every tool call then fails validation against the tool's real schema and the model retries with another invented name in a tight loop. With `openclaw_agent_consult` enabled (e.g. for voice agents that need CRM/memory access mid-call), this manifests as the agent going silent after asking a tool-needing question.

Per Google's [FunctionDeclaration spec](https://ai.google.dev/api/caching#FunctionDeclaration), `parameters` (Schema, OpenAPI 3.0.3) and `parametersJsonSchema` (raw JSON Schema) are **mutually exclusive**. Both are valid, but `parameters` is the older, universally-honored field, while `parametersJsonSchema` is honored only by some newer models. Our `RealtimeVoiceTool.parameters` shape (`type: "object"` with `properties`/`required`) is already a valid OpenAPI 3.0.3 Schema subset, so it works in both — only `parameters` is honored everywhere.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/google/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts` — 16/16 pass (existing connect-config assertion updated to expect `parameters`, providing regression coverage)
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` — file is clean (other extensions/core errors are pre-existing on `main` and unrelated: `extensions/discord` carbon, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner` websocket-cached transport, `src/media/qr-runtime` qrcode-tui)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on touched files — clean

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: With `parametersJsonSchema`, Gemini Live's preview audio models silently ignore the tool parameter schema and invent random parameter names on every `openclaw_agent_consult` tool call, causing every consult to instant-fail with `{"error":"question required"}` and the live phone agent to go silent on any tool-needing question.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw gateway 2026.5.7 running locally on Linux 6.17 (Ryzen AI Max+ 395), patched `voice-call` plugin in realtime mode, `provider: "telnyx"`, `realtime.provider: "google"` → Gemini Live model `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025`, real Telnyx number `+1 (651) 422-0083` placing/receiving real PSTN calls from a real US cell phone. ngrok tunnel `stretch-unclaimed-foe.ngrok-free.dev` for the realtime WebSocket and webhook surface.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Restart gateway with patched `extensions/google/realtime-voice-provider.ts` so `buildFunctionDeclarations` emits `parameters` instead of `parametersJsonSchema`: `openclaw gateway restart`
2. Place a live inbound call to the Telnyx number from a real cell phone
3. Ask a tool-needing question: "Look up prospect Laura Dunbar", "How many prospects are currently in the system?", "Search for Dennis Batty", "Who is Ryan Boog?"
4. Tail the gateway log: `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway -f | grep "realtime tool"`

**Evidence after fix**: Redacted runtime logs from the gateway during the live calls — `[voice-call] realtime tool call` / `realtime tool result` lines from `extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts:executeToolCall` (the patched logging surface). Log lines below are copied verbatim from `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway`:

Before this patch (sent as `parametersJsonSchema`) — the model invents field names and every consult instant-fails in 8–28 ms with `error=question required`:

```
[voice-call] realtime tool call: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=3c034b84-c776-43b7-8bc6-fe6bd9f4cd2f args={"query":"Look up prospect Laura Dunbar"}
[voice-call] realtime tool result: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=3c034b84-c776-43b7-8bc6-fe6bd9f4cd2f ms=11 result={"error":"question required"}
[voice-call] realtime tool call: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=9a10234f-4d07-4a33-9862-aef5f5cf7d60 args={"description":"Search for prospect named Laura Dunbar."}
[voice-call] realtime tool result: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=9a10234f-4d07-4a33-9862-aef5f5cf7d60 ms=28 result={"error":"question required"}
```

After this patch (sent as `parameters`) — the model honors the schema, uses the declared `question` field, and consults complete in 3.5–9 s with real openclaw agent answers:

```
[voice-call] realtime tool call: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=ac17e818-6054-4237-8565-387deabed891 args={"question":"How many prospects are currently in the system?"}
[voice-call] realtime tool result: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=ac17e818-6054-4237-8565-387deabed891 ms=4493 result={"text":"I currently have 67 prospects in the system, though one is a test entry and should be ignored."}
[voice-call] realtime tool call: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=ac17e818-6054-4237-8565-387deabed891 args={"question":"Search for prospect with the name 'Dennis Batty' (B-A-T-T-Y)."}
[voice-call] realtime tool result: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=ac17e818-6054-4237-8565-387deabed891 ms=3472 result={"text":"Yes, I found Dennis Batty of Dennis Batty & Associates. They are listed under Architecture."}
[voice-call] realtime tool call: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=ac17e818-6054-4237-8565-387deabed891 args={"question":"What information is available about Ryan Boog?"}
[voice-call] realtime tool result: name=openclaw_agent_consult callId=ac17e818-6054-4237-8565-387deabed891 ms=5094 result={"text":"Ryan Boog is the owner of Happy Dog Web Productions and Hoist..."}
```

**Observed result after fix**: Live phone caller hears Rosellen ask the question, hears the working-on-it filler immediately, and hears a real CRM-backed answer 3.5–9 seconds later. The model uses the schema's declared `question` field on every consult call instead of `query`/`description`/`prompt`. Voice turn latency on chitchat (no consult) remains sub-second. No regressions on the Twilio realtime path during the same session.

**What was not tested**: Non-preview Gemini Live model variants (only the preview `gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio-preview-12-2025` was tested — `parameters` works for it; `parametersJsonSchema` was the silent-ignore path); OpenAI Realtime path (uses a separate provider plugin not touched by this PR).

## Sources

- [Google FunctionDeclaration spec](https://ai.google.dev/api/caching#FunctionDeclaration) — `parametersJsonSchema` is documented as "mutually exclusive with parameters".

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: Harden ACP worker dispatch controls
target.body:
## Summary

- inject Claude and Gemini ACP model choices at process launch instead of replaying unsupported runtime config after session start
- skip unsupported Claude/Gemini runtime config replays for model, thinking/effort, and timeout controls
- allow `sessions_yield` to succeed when the gateway has an agent session context but no in-turn `onYield` callback

## Context

This fixes a Foreman dispatch failure mode where a spawned ACP worker was accepted, then the child agent failed shortly after start with `ACP_TURN_FAILED` while the parent believed the worker was still running. For Claude ACP, the launcher can now bake the requested model into the subprocess environment and avoid the post-init `setModel` path. For Gemini ACP, the requested model is passed with `--model` at launch.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): onModelDiagnosticEvent for trusted model.* events
target.body:
## Why

Plugins subscribing to diagnostic events via `onDiagnosticEvent` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime`) never see the `model.*` lifecycle. The public API filter at [`src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879-886`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts#L879-L886):

```ts
export function onDiagnosticEvent(listener: (evt: DiagnosticEventPayload) => void): () => void {
  return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
    if (metadata.trusted || event.type === "log.record") {
      return;
    }
    listener(event);
  });
}
```

drops **every** event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. The runtime emits `model.call.started`, `model.call.completed`, `model.call.error`, `model.usage`, and `model.failover` via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent`. So the entire model lifecycle is invisible to plugins.

This breaks observability/cost-attribution plugins. A telemetry bridge plugin needs:

- `model.call.completed` (provider/model identity) to populate per-call span attributes
- `model.usage` (token counts, cost) for per-call attribution

Today both subscriptions are dead code against the trusted emit paths. Concretely, the embedded codex/Responses runner emits the full model lifecycle via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent` (see `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts`), but no plugin listener ever receives any of it — model traffic on the dashboard side ends up bucketed as "unknown" instead of `gpt-5.5`/etc.

## Approach

Add a focused, opt-in plugin-sdk API `onModelDiagnosticEvent` that exposes exactly the five model lifecycle event types — and only those — to plugins. The general `onDiagnosticEvent` filter is unchanged: it still drops trusted events, so the safety contract of the broad subscription is preserved. Plugins that want model telemetry have to opt in explicitly.

The new helper otherwise mirrors `onDiagnosticEvent`'s contract (cloned-and-frozen payloads, isolated listener failures, unsubscribe disposer).

A module-local allowlist (`PLUGIN_VISIBLE_TRUSTED_MODEL_EVENT_TYPES`) documents which event types are reachable through this API and is the single place to update if more model lifecycle types are added later.

## Files

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts` — add `onModelDiagnosticEvent` + allowlist (+45 lines)
- `src/plugin-sdk/{diagnostic-runtime,index,compat}.ts` — re-export the new function so plugins can `import { onModelDiagnosticEvent } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime"` (or the top-level barrel / compat subpath)
- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` — three new tests:
  1. delivers all five `model.*` trusted types and filters non-model traffic
  2. the original `onDiagnosticEvent` still hides model events from plugins (defends the safety contract)
  3. unsubscribe disposer stops further delivery
- `CHANGELOG.md` — Unreleased / Changes entry

Total: 6 files, +158 / −2.

## Tests

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts`: 22/22 passing
- Plugin-SDK guardrail tests (`src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts`, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts`): 44 tests, all passing

## End-to-end validation

Built a full `dist/` from this branch, swapped it into a deployed gateway, and ran a real codex (`openai-codex/gpt-5.5`) turn from a Telegram-driven session. Bridge plugin logs show **both** subscriptions on startup:

```
[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
```

…and during the turn the bridge now receives the `model.usage` event for codex/gpt-5.5 (previously dead-code path):

```
[agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
```

The downstream dashboard bucket that had been showing "unknown" for every codex turn now correctly attributes to `gpt-5.5`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Plugins subscribing via the public `onDiagnosticEvent` API never observe trusted `model.*` lifecycle events (`model.call.started/completed/error`, `model.usage`, `model.failover`) because the existing filter at `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879` drops every event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. On my deployed install, this surfaced as Codex `gpt-5.5` turns bucketing as "unknown" in a downstream observability bridge plugin (agentweave-bridge) — the per-turn span never received `prov.llm.{provider,model}` because the bridge's `model.usage` / `model.call.completed` handlers were dead code against the trusted emit paths.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw v2026.5.10-beta.1 gateway running under `systemctl --user` on Linux (Debian 12, Node 24.13 via nvm) on my NAS (192.168.1.70), driving a Codex subscription session via Telegram channel and the embedded pi-runner. Local install built from this PR head: `dist/` produced by `pnpm build` from commit 52f87ce58a, swapped into `/home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/`, gateway restarted. Bridge plugin installed under `~/.openclaw/user-plugins/agentweave-bridge/`, rebuilt against the new SDK export.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```
  # 1. Build the patched openclaw from this branch
  cd /home/Arnab/dev/openclaw-model-usage
  CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  CI=true pnpm build

  # 2. Install over the canonical openclaw dist/
  systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway
  mv /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist.bak
  cp -r dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist
  systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway

  # 3. Confirm trust-gated body in the built artifact
  grep -A2 "function onModelDiagnosticEvent" \
    /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/diagnostic-events-*.js

  # 4. Drive a real Codex turn through Telegram and observe the bridge plugin logs
  journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway -f | grep agentweave-bridge
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  - Built-artifact contains the trust-gated callback:
    ```
    function onModelDiagnosticEvent(listener) {
      return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
        if (!metadata.trusted) return;
    ```
  - Gateway startup logs from `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` show the plugin subscribing to BOTH streams (previously only one):
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] OTel SDK started, exporting to: http://192.168.1.70:30418/v1/traces
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
    ```
  - During a real Telegram-initiated Codex turn (openai-codex/gpt-5.5), the bridge plugin now receives a trusted `model.usage` event that was previously invisible to it:
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] started root span for main session: agent:main:main agent: nix-v1
    [agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
    [agentweave-bridge] ended root span for session: agent:main:main
    ```
- Observed result after fix: The downstream observability dashboard that had been bucketing every Codex turn as "unknown" model now correctly attributes them to `gpt-5.5`, with cost figures flowing through the bridge's pricing fallback. No behavior change for any other plugin: the broad `onDiagnosticEvent` subscription still filters every trusted event as before (defended by the regression test `onDiagnosticEvent still hides model.* trusted events from plugins`). A separate regression test confirms forged untrusted `model.*` events from `emitDiagnosticEvent` do NOT reach the new opt-in subscription.
- What was not tested: I did not exercise this against an isolated subagent (`agent:isolated:*`) session, only `agent:main:main`. Did not run on macOS or Windows; tested under Linux Node 24.13 only. Did not run the live `pnpm test` matrix exhaustively — the four review-required test files all pass locally (`src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` 23/23, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-index.test.ts` 5/5, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts` 13/13, `src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts` 8/8) plus `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` and `pnpm build` are clean.

target.title: fix(doctor): suppress --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor` (without `--fix`) prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on every run, even when nothing in the run actually had a pending config change. After a successful `--fix` settles the workspace, a follow-up plain `doctor` invocation still emits the trailer, training operators to ignore a non-actionable cue.
- Why it matters: the trailer is the "you have something to apply" signal. When it fires on a clean state, it makes `Doctor complete.` ambiguous — users either re-run `--fix` for nothing or learn to ignore the line everywhere, including the cases where it does mean something.
- What changed: thread the already-tracked `pendingChanges` flag from `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` through the doctor config result, and gate the trailer in `runWriteConfigHealth` on it. The trailer now only emits when the config flow actually flagged at least one pending repair the user could apply.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no behavior change for `--fix` runs, no behavior change when `pendingChanges` is truly set, no edits to other doctor panels or contributions, no rename/move.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX (CLI diagnostics output)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #80435
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: the unconditional `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` trailer that `runWriteConfigHealth` emits whenever `--fix` is not passed, regardless of whether anything was actually pending.
- Real environment tested: local macOS (Darwin 24.6.0, Node v24.10.0) checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` at `upstream/main`, run with `pnpm dev doctor`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:changed` (lint core + tsgo core + tsgo core tests + import-cycles + boundary guards)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --fix --non-interactive` (× 2 to settle)
  - `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-trailer-test pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` (the previously failing invocation)
- Evidence after fix:
  - Unit lane: `Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 11 passed (11)` — includes the two new regression cases for `doctor:write-config`.
  - `pnpm check:changed`: exits 0 with `Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.` on lint, clean typecheck on `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:core:test`, `0 runtime value cycle(s)` from `check:import-cycles`.
  - Settled clean re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.
- Observed result after fix: on the clean re-run, the trailer is gone; runs that genuinely have pending repairs queued continue to print it (covered by the second new unit test).
- What was not tested: I did not reproduce the exact source-checkout + entrypoint-mismatch host layout from the bug report on this machine (no spare gateway-install/migration setup), so the live trailer-on-clean-state observation comes from a fresh `OPENCLAW_HOME` rather than the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade scenario. The function-level regression test mirrors the same code path either way.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `runWriteConfigHealth` reached its `else` branch whenever `shouldWriteConfig` resolved to `false`, and then emitted the trailer based only on whether `--fix` was missing. There was no signal of whether any earlier health contribution would have *had* something to apply, so the message printed even on a fully settled run.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no unit test asserting that the trailer is suppressed on a clean run. The new `doctor:write-config — skips the doctor --fix trailer when no pending config changes remain` test closes that gap.
- Contributing context (if known): `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` already tracks `pendingChanges` locally for `finalizeDoctorConfigFlow`, but it wasn't surfaced on the returned `DoctorConfigResult`, so downstream contributions had no way to consult it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - `doctor:write-config` skips the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === false` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
  - `doctor:write-config` still prints the `--fix` trailer when `configResult.pendingChanges === true` and `prompter.shouldRepair === false`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely in the single branch in `runWriteConfigHealth`; exercising both `pendingChanges` values against the live `doctor:write-config` contribution is enough to lock in the gate without standing up the whole doctor flow.
- Existing test that already covers this: none — the existing tests in this file assert ordering and the legacy update-skip helper, not trailer emission.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw doctor` (no `--fix`) no longer prints `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` on runs where no health contribution flagged a pending config change. Runs that do have pending changes are unchanged.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."

After:
doctor --non-interactive (clean state) -> Doctor complete.
doctor --non-interactive (pending change) -> Doctor complete. + "Run openclaw doctor --fix to apply changes."
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (Darwin 24.6.0), reporter saw it on Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.10.0 (reporter: Node v22.22.2)
- Model/provider: N/A — pure doctor CLI diagnostics
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): a settled `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` where the previous `--fix` exited without `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`

### Steps

1. `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive` until it exits cleanly with no `Updated ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` line.
2. `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` immediately afterward.
3. Observe the trailer.

### Expected

The settled re-run ends with `└  Doctor complete.` and no trailing `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` line.

### Actual (pre-fix)

The settled re-run ends with `Run "openclaw doctor --fix" to apply changes.` followed by `└  Doctor complete.`, even though no panel reported a pending change.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing-then-passing unit assertions covering both `pendingChanges` branches (`src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`).
- [x] Trace/log snippet from `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` on settled `OPENCLAW_HOME` showing `Doctor complete.` with no trailer.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Settled clean re-run of `pnpm dev doctor --non-interactive` against a freshly populated `OPENCLAW_HOME` ends with `Doctor complete.` and no trailer.
  - `pnpm test src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` — 11 passed, including the two new cases.
  - `pnpm check:changed` — green on lint core, runtime sidecar loader guard, import-cycles, webhook body guard, pairing guards, changelog attributions, dup coverage, `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:core:test`, oxlint core.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `pendingChanges === undefined` (older test mocks / future callers) — falsy, trailer suppressed; backwards compatible with existing inline-context tests in this file.
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === false` — trailer still prints (covered by the second new unit test).
  - `pendingChanges === true && shouldRepair === true` — function takes the write branch, never reaches the trailer.
- What I did **not** verify: a live reproduction of the original Ubuntu 24.04 aarch64 layout with `auditGatewayServiceConfig` returning a non-empty issue list, because that requires the reporter's npm-over-pnpm upgrade setup.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — `pendingChanges` is added as an optional field on `DoctorConfigResult`; existing mocks/test fixtures that omit it observe the previous "no trailer" outcome they already had when nothing was pending.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a future contribution might want the trailer to fire on a state that isn't surfaced via `configResult.pendingChanges`.
  - Mitigation: any new pending state should be threaded through `loadAndMaybeMigrateDoctorConfig` (now trivial — just OR it into the existing local `pendingChanges`). The regression test locks the contract for `runWriteConfigHealth`.

AI-assisted: drafted with Claude Code; human-run real behavior proof and unit test verification on local checkout.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(mattermost): add automatic ack reactions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost supported typing, preview streaming, and manual `message action=react`, but it did not send automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- Why it matters: Mattermost was the main chat-channel outlier versus Discord, Slack, Telegram, Matrix, and WhatsApp for the baseline "seen / working on it" feedback path.
- What changed: added Mattermost automatic ack reactions, Mattermost emoji-name normalization for common unicode defaults, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` / `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides, and the matching generated config-surface artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Mattermost lifecycle status reactions, no changes to typing behavior, and no changes to draft preview streaming.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw maintainer worktree on Linux with Mattermost-focused tests, build, generated-config checks, the full changed-scope gate, and a live Mattermost workspace smoke on this exact branch.

- What was not tested: exhaustive live coverage for uncommon/custom unicode emoji names.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
After:
```

target.title: refactor(auto-reply): drop dead /reset branches in fast-path session init
target.body:
## Summary

This PR was originally proposed as a fix for #80377, claiming `/reset` was preserving session-level overrides incorrectly. After review by `clawsweeper` and inspection of `initSessionState` (the actual production reset path), it turns out the runtime behavior is **intentional**:

- behavior overrides (thinking, verbose, reasoning, ttsAuto) are deliberately preserved across `/reset` so users don't re-enable them every time (see `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts:510-518`)
- model/provider/auth overrides go through `resolveResetPreservedSelection`, which preserves user-driven selections and clears only auto-created ones (per #69301)

So this PR has been **pivoted from a behavior fix to a cleanup-only refactor**. The original commit (`1a1685a`) and the regression tests it added have been reverted in `1d70235`. The only remaining net change versus `origin/main` is replacing nine trivial `resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.X : existingEntry?.X` ternaries in `initFastReplySessionState` with the simple `existingEntry?.X` expression.

**Net effect: zero semantic change vs `origin/main`.** The dead `resetTriggered` branches no longer mislead readers into thinking the fast-path bootstrap has reset-specific logic that the runtime doesn't actually have.

Issue #80377 should be closed as wontfix / by-design (the bundle pattern that was reported is a copy-paste artifact in this fast-path init, not a production runtime bug). This PR does **not** fix #80377; it only references it.

## Files touched

- `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-fast-path.ts` — simplify 9 no-op ternaries (no semantic change)

## Behavior proof (no-op refactor)

This is a semantic no-op cleanup; the proof is the diff itself. Before/after, every assignment evaluates to the same value:

```ts
// Before (one of nine identical patterns)
thinkingLevel: resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.thinkingLevel : existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
// After
thinkingLevel: existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
```

For both `resetTriggered === true` and `resetTriggered === false`, both expressions yield `existingEntry?.thinkingLevel`. The `resetTriggered` semantics still flow through the `...(!resetTriggered ? existingEntry : undefined)` spread on line 245 (unchanged), so reset still drops `responseUsage` and `sessionStartedAt` correctly. The full pre-existing test suite for `get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` (12 tests) continues to pass:

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  12 passed (12)
```

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` → 12/12 (pre-existing tests, unchanged)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` → green
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` → green (with `GOMEMLIMIT=2GiB` on a memory-constrained dev box)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(gateway): disable startup trace monitor on startup failure
target.body:
## Summary
- add an explicit `startupTrace.close()` cleanup path in gateway startup tracing
- call `startupTrace.close()` in the startup failure catch path so `monitorEventLoopDelay` is always disabled on failed startup
- keep current extension type tests compiling after latest `main` changed stricter `Response` and regexp expectations

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway startup tracing with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` no longer leaves the event-loop delay monitor enabled when startup fails before readiness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local Linux checkout of this PR branch at `e81371fe6a0bbc8a84d5c1a51a7a821611f268fa`, running Node through the repo's `tsx` loader against a real temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created a temporary invalid `openclaw.json`, then ran:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$tmpdir/openclaw.json" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 timeout 20s node --import tsx -e 'import("./src/gateway/server.impl.ts").then(async ({ startGatewayServer }) => { try { await startGatewayServer(0, { bind: "loopback", controlUiEnabled: false }); console.log("unexpected startup success"); process.exitCode = 2; } catch (error) { console.log("startup failed as expected"); console.log(String(error instanceof Error ? error.message : error).split("\n")[0]); } })'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
  ```text
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.file 0.5ms total=26.7ms eventLoopMax=9.9ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.hash 0.2ms total=47.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read.parse 0.3ms total=49.3ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot.read 28.1ms total=53.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  [gateway] startup trace: config.snapshot 30.6ms total=55.1ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
  startup failed as expected
  Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-proof-4texI2/openclaw.json.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The `node --import tsx` gateway startup process exited with status `0` under the `timeout 20s` wrapper after reporting the expected config failure, which shows the startup trace monitor did not keep the process alive after the failed startup path.
- **What was not tested**: Full packaged `openclaw gateway run` startup was not rerun because this source checkout does not contain built `dist/entry.js`; this proof directly imports the gateway server implementation from the PR checkout instead.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm test src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server.impl.ts extensions/googlechat/src/targets.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: [codex] Further optimize Telegram media and reply context flow
target.body:
## Summary

- add scoped indexes to the Telegram message cache so `recentBefore` and `around` avoid scanning and sorting the full cache
- cache successful Telegram media resolutions by file identity and relevant runtime options
- revalidate cached media paths before reuse so TTL cleanup cannot leave stale attachments behind
- add regression coverage for thread-scoped chat windows, media cache hits, and stale media cache eviction
- bump `@openclaw/fs-safe` to 0.2.1 so focused Telegram tests can install and resolve `@openclaw/fs-safe/config` cleanly

## Context

This builds on earlier Telegram-route performance work by `obviyus`, further optimizing the same flow by tightening reply-context lookups and avoiding repeated Telegram media hydration.

## Why

Telegram reply-context building can repeatedly query recent chat windows and hydrate the same reply media. This PR keeps those hot paths bounded while preserving the existing chat-wide and thread-specific behavior.

The media cache also checks the saved file still exists before returning a cached path, because long-running gateways can remove saved media through TTL cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reply-context lookups stay thread-scoped or chat-wide as requested, repeated Telegram media hydration reuses the cached saved path, and a deleted saved media file is evicted and re-downloaded instead of returning a stale path.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw gateway from this PR head (`b8ad83191132ea2cdb1c9a40312ded58f58444ba`) on macOS, running `OpenClaw 2026.5.10-beta.1` with the Telegram plugin loaded from the PR checkout. Telegram ingress used the real Bot API webhook for `@acecoderbot` through a temporary Cloudflare tunnel. The LLM provider was mocked only to make the response deterministic; Telegram update delivery, reply-context hydration, message-cache persistence, and reply dispatch all ran through the PR checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm openclaw --log-level debug gateway run --port 19043 --auth token --token [redacted] --bind loopback --verbose --ws-log compact` from the PR checkout with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a real Telegram Bot API webhook, and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible provider. Sent `PR80243 live seed OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_1` to `@acecoderbot`, then sent a real Telegram reply to that DM with `PR80243 live reply-context OPENCLAW_E2E_OK_2`. Also ran a local media/cache proof through `node --import tsx` using a real temporary state directory and local trusted Bot API file path.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live runtime logs and persisted cache/model-input excerpts from the actual PR gateway run:


Supplemental local media/cache output:

```text

```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live Telegram reply reached OpenClaw with `reply_to_message`, OpenClaw logged `telegram reply-context` for `replyToId=10034`, the inbound prompt included `has_reply_context: true`, the nearby reply target window included both the original DM and the reply, and both Telegram messages completed through the PR gateway. The supplemental cache/media proof showed thread-scoped windows stayed scoped, chat-wide windows included cross-topic history, the second media resolution reused the cached path without another `getFile`, and stale saved-path eviction forced a fresh `getFile`.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps for the live Telegram reply-context behavior. The media cache proof used OpenClaw's real resolver and media store with a local trusted Bot API file path rather than downloading a large external Telegram media file during this proof run.

## Validation

target.title: feat(discord): add implicit reply mention policy
target.body:
Closes #80234.

Adds `channels.discord.implicitReplyMentions`:

```json5
{
  fromUsers: true,
  fromBots: true
}
```

Defaults preserve current behavior. Multi-bot deployments can set `fromBots: false` so bot-authored native replies do not wake another bot via the implicit `reply_to_bot` path; explicit `<@botId>` mentions still work.

Checks run:

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Discord native reply-only mentions can wake an OpenClaw bot when another bot replies to one of its messages. This PR adds a policy knob so deployments can keep human reply activation while disabling bot-authored reply-only activation.

Real environment tested: Private Discord server with a private test channel and two OpenClaw Discord bot accounts. Server, channel, bot names, message IDs, and account IDs are intentionally omitted from this public proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. In the private test channel, send an anchor message from the target bot.
2. Send a native Discord reply from a separate relay bot to that anchor message. The reply body contains no textual `<@bot>` mention.
3. Observe whether the target bot wakes/responds.
4. Repeat after applying this branch with `implicitReplyMentions: { fromUsers: true, fromBots: false }`.
5. Send a separate control message from the relay bot that contains a real textual `<@bot>` mention and asks the target bot to reply with a known marker.

Evidence after fix: Redacted live output excerpt from the private Discord test channel:

```text
after-fix native reply-only test:
relay bot | "oc-proof-80235-after4-231018 native-reply-no-explicit-mention" | ref=<target-bot-anchor>
UNEXPECTED_TARGET_RESPONSES=0

explicit mention control:
target bot | "oc-proof-80235-control-231107 explicit-mention-ok" | ref=<relay-bot-control-message>
EXPLICIT_CONTROL_OK=true
```

Observed result after fix: With `fromBots:false`, the bot-authored native reply-only message did not wake the target bot during the observation window. A real textual explicit mention still woke the target bot and produced the requested marker response.

What was not tested: Human-authored reply-only behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof. PluralKit behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof.

I did not run a live gateway flow in this read-only review.

target.title: fix: propagate gateway TLS fingerprints to bootstrap clients
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway clients bootstrapped outside `callGateway` (ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling) did not receive the local TLS certificate fingerprint for a self-signed local WSS gateway.
- Why it matters: those clients could fail TLS verification even when the local gateway bootstrap had enough information to trust the generated certificate.
- What changed: added a shared TLS fingerprint resolver, reused it from `callGateway`, propagated `bootstrap.tlsFingerprint` into ACP/MCP/approval `GatewayClient` construction, and added regression coverage for local, remote, override, and non-TLS cases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway server TLS behavior, auth mode, config schema, environment variable, or public endpoint changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACP bootstrap can reach a locally configured TLS gateway using the bootstrapped local TLS fingerprint instead of failing certificate verification.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, temporary config/state under `/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke`, self-signed local WSS gateway on `127.0.0.1:19791`, auth disabled for the smoke.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19791 --ws-log compact

OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791

printf 'exit\n' | \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev
```

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal/log output):

```text
Gateway Health
OK (27ms)

[gateway] http server listening ...
[gateway] ready
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ health ...

ACP output reached the gateway connection path and then stopped at the expected local scope approval gate:
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
ACP bridge failed: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
```

- Observed result after fix: local TLS health succeeded, and ACP reached the gateway authorization path without a TLS/self-signed certificate or fingerprint mismatch.
- What was not tested: a full ACP prompt turn after approving the scope upgrade in the temporary local gateway.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug was covered by the new regression tests rather than a saved failing runtime log.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: local TLS fingerprint selection lived inside `callGateway`, while shared bootstrap resolution only returned URL/auth/timeout data. ACP, MCP channel bridge, and operator approval clients constructed `GatewayClient` without the local TLS fingerprint.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted that bootstrap clients forward a resolved TLS fingerprint into `GatewayClient`.
- Contributing context (if known): remote-mode and environment URL overrides needed explicit precedence rules so remote pins are applied only to TLS remote targets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts`, `src/acp/server.startup.test.ts`, `src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: local TLS bootstrap returns and forwards the local fingerprint; explicit CLI fingerprints win; remote TLS fingerprints are inherited only for TLS remote targets; non-TLS targets do not receive TLS pins.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver and each client construction seam without needing a long-lived gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/call.test.ts` covered `callGateway` behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Local self-signed TLS gateway clients launched through ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling now inherit the same trusted fingerprint behavior that `callGateway` already had.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
resolve bootstrap -> GatewayClient(url, token) -> local WSS cert verification can fail

After:
resolve bootstrap -> resolve TLS fingerprint -> GatewayClient(url, token, tlsFingerprint) -> local WSS trust succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development machine
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm local checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway local WSS, ACP client smoke
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary config with `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.tls.enabled=true`, `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true`, `gateway.bind=loopback`, `gateway.port=19791`

### Steps

1. Start a temporary local TLS gateway with self-signed auto-generated certs.
2. Run `pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791` against that gateway.
3. Start `pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev` against the same temporary config.
4. Run targeted gateway/ACP/MCP regression tests and changed-file checks.
5. Run Codex review against `origin/main` and address the findings.

### Expected

- Bootstrap clients receive the correct TLS fingerprint for the selected gateway target.
- Local WSS bootstrap does not fail due to the self-signed gateway certificate.
- Remote TLS pins are not applied to plain `ws://` targets or local fallback targets.

### Actual

- `gateway health` returned `OK` over local WSS.
- ACP reached the gateway authorization path and stopped at the expected scope upgrade approval gate, with no TLS/fingerprint failure.
- Targeted tests and changed-file checks passed.
- Codex review reported no remaining actionable correctness issues after two review fixes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation commands run locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test \
  src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts \
  src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts \
  src/gateway/call.test.ts \
  src/acp/server.startup.test.ts \
  src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts

pnpm check:changed
git diff --check
codex review --base origin/main
```

Results:

```text
3 Vitest shards, 114 tests passed
pnpm check:changed passed
git diff --check passed
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local TLS fingerprint selection, explicit fingerprint precedence, remote WSS fingerprint inheritance, `ws://` remote target exclusion, remote-mode local fallback choosing local TLS fingerprint, ACP/MCP/approval client propagation seams.
- Edge cases checked: CLI override vs config-derived URL, env URL override, remote WSS, remote plain WS, missing remote URL fallback, no TLS config.
- What you did **not** verify: a full ACP prompt turn after manually approving the temporary local scope upgrade.

AI-assisted implementation note: this patch was implemented with OpenClaw/Codex assistance, and Codex review was run locally before PR creation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: applying a remote TLS fingerprint to the wrong target could break local fallback or non-TLS gateway connections.
  - Mitigation: the shared resolver only applies remote fingerprints to selected TLS remote targets, with regression tests for `ws://` and missing-remote-url fallback behavior.

target.title: fix(browser): time out remote tab enumeration
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #58968 by bounding remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration with the existing remote CDP timeout budget.
- Evicts the scoped cached/in-flight Playwright connection on enumeration timeout so the next request can reconnect instead of reusing a stuck pipe.
- Adds regression coverage for a stuck page-target lookup and verifies remote profile tab listing passes the resolved timeout into the Playwright path.

## Problem
Remote CDP profiles use a persistent Playwright connection for tab listing because HTTP `/json/list` is ephemeral for those providers. When that Playwright-backed enumeration stalls, browser requests can hang behind the stuck CDP operation instead of failing fast.

## Root Cause
`createProfileTabOps().readTabs()` passed only `cdpUrl` and SSRF policy to `listPagesViaPlaywright()`. The lower-level Playwright enumeration path had no operation-level timeout around `connectBrowser()`, `getAllPages()`, or per-page target/title/url reads, and a stuck scoped connection could remain cached or in-flight.

## Architectural Reasoning
The fix reuses the existing browser remote CDP timeout contract rather than adding a new config key. Remote tab listing now uses `max(remoteCdpTimeoutMs, remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs)`, which matches the existing distinction between HTTP discovery and WebSocket handshake budgets. Timeout cleanup goes through the existing scoped Playwright disconnect helper, preserving per-CDP URL isolation and avoiding hidden coupling to other browser profiles.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration now returns a bounded timeout error instead of waiting indefinitely on a stalled CDP discovery/enumeration path.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 worktree, Node `v22.15.0`, `pnpm 10.33.2`, branch `hemant/issue-triage-20260509`, commit `9aba9b405ae5c4601211fd0b2b428d9640425b8b`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a real loopback HTTP endpoint that accepts CDP discovery connections and deliberately never responds; called production `listPagesViaPlaywright()` through `node --import tsx` with `timeoutMs: 100` and an SSRF policy allowing loopback/private access; closed the scoped Playwright connection and destroyed the held socket after the call.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```console
$ node --import tsx - < hanging-cdp-smoke.mjs
{"ok":true,"error":"Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms","elapsedMs":309}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production browser module returned `Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms` and the script exited cleanly instead of hanging on the held CDP request.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a real remote Chrome host because the failure mode is a stalled CDP endpoint; the local loopback endpoint exercises the same production timeout path with deterministic cleanup. Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` still has unrelated Windows environment failures noted below.

## Testing
- `pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.tab-ops.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` from `O:\` subst drive to avoid the repo path-with-spaces launcher bug
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` from `O:\`
- `pnpm lint:extensions` from `O:\`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` exited 0 with no lane output

Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` was also run locally. It failed on unrelated Windows environment assumptions: local browser auto-detection / missing supported browser launch fixtures and existing `/tmp` path expectations being normalized to `C:\tmp`. The two touched regression test files pass.

## Risk Analysis
Risk is medium because this touches async browser/CDP lifecycle behavior. The change is scoped to remote Playwright tab enumeration, does not alter local managed browser JSON tab listing, and does not change navigation, action, or screenshot behavior. On timeout, OpenClaw drops only the scoped Playwright connection for that CDP URL and leaves the actual remote browser tabs intact.

## Backward Compatibility
No config, API, or response-shape changes. Existing `browser.remoteCdpTimeoutMs` and `browser.remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs` now also bound remote Playwright tab enumeration.

## Screenshots / Logs
The real behavior proof above is terminal output from the after-fix smoke; no UI screenshot is applicable.

target.title: fix(skills): warn for untrusted local sources
target.body:
Fixes #57535

## Problem summary
Local skills from workspace, `.agents`, managed, and extra skill roots were shown as ordinary ready/blocked skills. Operators had no first-class status signal distinguishing ClawHub/bundled skills from local skill files that should be reviewed before enablement or invocation.

## Root cause
The skills loader already tracks each skill source, and ClawHub installs already write `.clawhub/origin.json`, but `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus` collapsed that into `source`/`bundled` only. CLI and Control UI status surfaces therefore had no trust metadata to render.

## Architectural reasoning
This keeps runtime loading semantics unchanged and adds trust classification at the status/reporting layer. Built-in skills are trusted as `openclaw-bundled`; valid ClawHub origin metadata is trusted as `clawhub`; reviewed local roots configured in `skills.load.trustedDirs` are trusted as `trusted-dir`; known local sources without either signal are marked `untrustedLocalSource` with a warning. The change is additive to the status API and avoids coupling core loader behavior to owner-specific skill ids.

## Testing performed
- `pnpm test src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/skills-status.ts src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.format.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts src/config/types.skills.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts ui/src/ui/types.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills-shared.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts docs/tools/skills.md docs/tools/skills-config.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed via a temporary `P:\` mapping to avoid the repo path-with-spaces issue.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm config:docs:check` initially detected expected config baseline drift; after `pnpm config:docs:gen`, `pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed after clean rebase.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed locally. Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable because `blacksmith` is not installed on PATH.

`pnpm test:changed` was also run locally and selected a broad `unit-fast` shard; it failed in 19 unrelated files with existing Windows/path/host assumptions such as `/tmp` becoming `C:\tmp`, POSIX table glyph expectations on Windows output, A2UI scaffold 404 in `extensions/canvas`, and temp SQLite cleanup `EBUSY`. The focused touched-surface tests above passed.

`pnpm format:docs:check` could not be used as proof in this environment because the wrapper crashed on Node v22.15.0 with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` in `scripts/format-docs.mjs`; targeted `oxfmt --check`, `lint:docs`, and `docs:check-mdx` passed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary workspace skill without ClawHub origin metadata is now classified as an untrusted local source.
- Real environment tested: Windows checkout on branch `hemant/57535-untrusted-skill-sources`, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `pnpm exec tsx` script from the repo root that created a temporary `<workspace>/skills/local-review/SKILL.md`, called `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus(workspace)`, and rendered `formatSkillsCheck(report, {})`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```json
{
  "name": "local-review",
  "source": "openclaw-workspace",
  "trustSource": "local",
  "untrustedLocalSource": true,
  "hasWarning": true
}
```

```text
Untrusted local sources
local-review
Add the directory to skills.load.trustedDirs only if you trust this workspace-owned skill source.
```

- Observed result after fix: Local skill loading still succeeded and the status/check surface warned before users enable or invoke the local skill.
- What was not tested: No browser screenshot was captured because this is a status/API/CLI/UI-rendering change covered by jsdom UI tests rather than a visual layout change.

## Risk analysis
Low-to-medium. The classification is additive and synchronous, using small local origin/trusted-dir checks during status generation. It does not alter skill discovery, precedence, eligibility, model visibility, command visibility, or execution. Main residual risk is users seeing new warnings for legitimate local/plugin-provided skill roots until they add reviewed directories to `skills.load.trustedDirs`.

## Backward compatibility
Backward-compatible. Existing skill config continues to load the same skills. New `SkillStatusEntry` fields are additive, and `skills.load.trustedDirs` is optional. Existing `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets` behavior is unchanged; docs now clarify that it controls loader containment while `trustedDirs` controls warning suppression.

## Screenshots/logs
No screenshots. Relevant command output is summarized above.

target.title: fix(gateway): add systemd watchdog heartbeat
target.body:
## Problem summary

Closes #11973. The generated systemd gateway unit had crash restart behavior, but it did not configure a watchdog heartbeat. If the gateway event loop wedged without exiting, systemd had no liveness signal to trigger recovery.

## Root cause

The existing gateway maintenance loop already emits a 30s keepalive tick, but that liveness signal only reaches OpenClaw clients. The systemd unit renderer did not opt into `WatchdogSec`, and the gateway runtime did not notify systemd through the `NOTIFY_SOCKET`/`systemd-notify` contract.

## Architectural reasoning

- Added a small `src/infra/systemd-notify.ts` helper that is inert unless `NOTIFY_SOCKET` is present, so non-systemd, Windows, macOS, foreground, and unmanaged gateway runs keep the same behavior.
- Reused `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` as the watchdog cadence source. This ties the systemd ping to the existing event-loop tick instead of creating a separate interval or lifecycle path.
- Kept the systemd unit on the existing default `Type=simple` behavior and only added `WatchdogSec=90s` plus `NotifyAccess=all`. The `NotifyAccess=all` line is needed because notifications are sent by the `systemd-notify` helper process inside the service cgroup.
- The notifier disables itself after the first `systemd-notify` failure and logs once, avoiding repeated subprocess failures while preserving the gateway's existing session, cleanup, and health loops.
- No gateway protocol, API, session, or backward-compatibility contract changes.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts` passed after rebase: 3 Vitest shards, 19 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/systemd-notify.ts src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md CHANGELOG.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs -- docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed from a subst drive path to avoid the local Windows path-with-space issue.
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` and `pnpm check:changed` exited 0 in this checkout, though they produced no lane output.

Known local environment limitations:

- Adding `src/daemon/systemd.test.ts` to the focused test command still shows pre-existing Windows-only failures unrelated to this change: `process.geteuid` is absent on Windows, and two env-file assertions expect an unquoted Windows path. The changed renderer test itself passes.
- `pnpm build` could not be completed in this Windows shell. The default `pnpm` wrapper is pinned to local Node v22.15.0 while the repo requires `>=22.16.0`; a subst-drive retry with bundled Node v24.14.0 gets past that but `build-all` still fails at the bootstrap guard because the expected generated `.js` dist aliases are missing in this environment. A direct `tsdown` build completed successfully, and generated hash drift was restored.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated systemd gateway units now include watchdog recovery settings, and the runtime notifier is a safe no-op outside systemd-managed services.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, branch `hemant/11973-systemd-watchdog`, Node v22.15.0 for focused proof commands.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm exec tsx` commands from the repo root to render the generated systemd unit and invoke the notifier with no `NOTIFY_SOCKET`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { buildSystemdUnit } from './src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts'; const unit = buildSystemdUnit({ description: 'OpenClaw Gateway', programArguments: ['/usr/bin/openclaw','gateway','run'], environment: {} }); console.log(unit.split('\n').filter((line) => ['Restart=always','RestartSec=5','WatchdogSec=90s','NotifyAccess=all','KillMode=control-group'].includes(line)).join('\n'));"
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
WatchdogSec=90s
NotifyAccess=all
KillMode=control-group

$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { createSystemdNotifier } from './src/infra/systemd-notify.ts'; void (async () => { const notifier = createSystemdNotifier({ env: {} }); await notifier.ready(); await notifier.watchdog(); console.log('systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok'); })();"
systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok
```

- Observed result after fix: The actual unit renderer includes the watchdog lines, and the notifier performs no work when the systemd notification socket is absent. Unmanaged/local gateway runs remain unaffected, while managed systemd units can receive liveness pings from the existing maintenance tick.
- What was not tested: A live Linux systemd user service restart on a hung event loop from this Windows workspace.

## Risk analysis

Medium-low. The unit settings affect systemd-managed gateway installs only. Runtime notifications are gated by `NOTIFY_SOCKET`, failure-disabled after the first subprocess error, and do not change gateway protocol/session flows. The main operational risk is a missing `systemd-notify` binary on a systemd host; in that case the helper logs once and leaves existing crash-restart behavior intact.

## Screenshots/logs

No UI screenshots. This is CLI/service generation behavior; relevant command output is included above.

## Backward compatibility

Backward compatible. Existing unmanaged gateway runs, existing installed units until regenerated, non-systemd platforms, gateway API behavior, and session/gateway protocol behavior are unchanged. Docs now show the watchdog lines alongside the generated unit settings.

target.title: fix(opencode-go): add supportedReasoningEfforts to DeepSeek V4 model entries
target.body:
## Problem

DeepSeek V4 Flash and V4 Pro natively support reasoning_effort: "max" at the API level. OpenClaw's stream layer already maps xhigh/max to "max" on the wire via resolveDeepSeekV4ReasoningEffort(). However, the opencode-go provider did not expose these levels through the thinking validation layer, causing /think xhigh and /think max to be rejected.

## Solution

Follow the same pattern used by the OpenRouter extension: add supportsXHighThinking and resolveThinkingProfile hooks to the opencode-go provider registration, declaring a thinking profile that includes xhigh and max for DeepSeek V4 models.

## Changes

- extensions/opencode-go/thinking-policy.ts (new) — Thinking level definitions for DeepSeek V4 models (off through max).
- extensions/opencode-go/index.ts — Added supportsXHighThinking and resolveThinkingProfile hooks.

## Real behavior proof

### behavior

Thinking level "max" was previously rejected for opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash with the error "Thinking level "max" is not supported". After the fix, setting thinkingDefault: "max" is accepted and the session activates with Think: max without errors.

### environment

- OpenClaw v2026.5.7 (eeef486)
- Provider: opencode-go
- Model: deepseek-v4-flash
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (Linux 6.8.0-111-generic x64)
- Running as systemd user service on a KVM VPS

### steps

1. Applied the equivalent patch to the compiled extension JS at dist/extensions/opencode-go/index.js, adding supportsXHighThinking and resolveThinkingProfile hooks matching the source change in this PR.
2. Set thinkingDefault: "max" in openclaw.json.
3. Restarted the OpenClaw gateway.
4. Verified session status shows Think: max active.
5. Verified no "not supported" errors from the thinking validation layer.

### evidence

Session status output:

```
Model: opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash
Think: max
```

Config:

```
"thinkingDefault": "max"
```

### observedResult

Think: max is accepted and active. The session applies thinking level "max" and the stream wrapper sends reasoning_effort: "max" on the wire for all API requests.

### notTested

Stream-level reasoning_effort payload was verified via code inspection of resolveDeepSeekV4ReasoningEffort() which maps both xhigh and max to the string "max". Actual wire capture was not performed.
