target.title: fix-79983-exec-policy
target.body:
---
summary: "Host exec approvals: policy knobs, allowlists, and the YOLO/strict workflow"
read_when:
  - Configuring exec approvals or allowlists
  - Implementing exec approval UX in the macOS app
  - Reviewing sandbox-escape prompts and their implications
title: "Exec approvals"
sidebarTitle: "Exec approvals"
---

Exec approvals are the **companion app / node host guardrail** for letting
a sandboxed agent run commands on a real host (`gateway` or `node`). A
safety interlock: commands are allowed only when policy + allowlist +
(optional) user approval all agree. Exec approvals stack **on top of**
tool policy and elevated gating (unless elevated is set to `full`, which
skips approvals).

For a mode-first overview of `deny`, `allowlist`, `ask`, `auto`, `full`,
Codex Guardian mapping, and ACPX harness permissions, see
[Permission modes](/tools/permission-modes).

<Note>
Effective policy is the **stricter** of `tools.exec.*` and approvals
defaults; if an approvals field is omitted, the `tools.exec` value is
used. Host exec also uses local approvals state on that machine - a
host-local `ask: "always"` in the execution host approvals file keeps
prompting even if session or config defaults request `ask: "on-miss"`.
</Note>

## Inspecting the effective policy

| Command                                                          | What it shows                                                                          |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `openclaw approvals get` / `--gateway` / `--node <id\|name\|ip>` | Requested policy, host policy sources, and the effective result.                       |
| `openclaw exec-policy show`                                      | Local-machine merged view.                                                             |
| `openclaw exec-policy set` / `preset`                            | Synchronize the local requested policy with the local host approvals file in one step. |

`openclaw exec-policy show` does not include per-session `/exec` overrides. Send
`/exec` in the target chat or cron session to inspect the host and policy that
the next agent turn will use.

When a local scope requests `host=node`, `exec-policy show` reports that
scope as node-managed at runtime instead of pretending the local
approvals file is the source of truth.

If the companion app UI is **not available**, any request that would
normally prompt is resolved by the **ask fallback** (default: `deny`).

<Tip>
Native chat approval clients can seed channel-specific affordances on the
pending approval message. For example, Matrix seeds reaction shortcuts
(`✅` allow once, `❌` deny, `♾️` allow always) while still leaving
`/approve ...` commands in the message as a fallback.
</Tip>

## Where it applies

Exec approvals are enforced locally on the execution host:

- **Gateway host** → `openclaw` process on the gateway machine.
- **Node host** → node runner (macOS companion app or headless node host).

### Trust model

- Gateway-authenticated callers are trusted operators for that Gateway.
- Paired nodes extend that trusted operator capability onto the node host.
- Exec approvals reduce accidental execution risk, but are **not** a per-user auth boundary or filesystem read-only policy.
- Once approved, a command can mutate files according to the selected host or sandbox filesystem permissions.
- Approved node-host runs bind canonical execution context: canonical cwd, exact argv, env binding when present, and pinned executable path when applicable.
- For shell scripts and direct interpreter/runtime file invocations, OpenClaw also tries to bind one concrete local file operand. If that bound file changes after approval but before execution, the run is denied instead of executing drifted content.
- File binding is intentionally best-effort, **not** a complete semantic model of every interpreter/runtime loader path. If approval mode cannot identify exactly one concrete local file to bind, it refuses to mint an approval-backed run instead of pretending full coverage.

### macOS split

- The **node host service** forwards `system.run` to the **macOS app** over local IPC.
- The **macOS app** enforces approvals and executes the command in UI context.

## Settings and storage

Approvals live in a local JSON file on the execution host. When
`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` is set, the file follows that state directory;
otherwise it uses the default OpenClaw state directory:

```text
$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/exec-approvals.json
# otherwise
~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json
```

The default approval socket follows the same root:
`$OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/exec-approvals.sock`, or
`~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.sock` when the variable is unset.

Example schema:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "socket": {
    "path": "~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.sock",
    "token": "base64url-token"
  },
  "defaults": {
    "security": "deny",
    "ask": "on-miss",
    "askFallback": "deny",
    "autoAllowSkills": false
  },
  "agents": {
    "main": {
      "security": "allowlist",
      "ask": "on-miss",
      "askFallback": "deny",
      "autoAllowSkills": true,
      "allowlist": [
        {
          "id": "B0C8C0B3-2C2D-4F8A-9A3C-5A4B3C2D1E0F",
          "pattern": "~/Projects/**/bin/rg",
          "source": "allow-always",
          "commandText": "rg -n TODO",
          "lastUsedAt": 1737150000000,
          "lastUsedCommand": "rg -n TODO",
          "lastResolvedPath": "/Users/user/Projects/.../bin/rg"
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

## Policy knobs

### `tools.exec.mode`

`tools.exec.mode` is the preferred normalized policy surface for host exec.
Values are:

- `deny` - block host exec.
- `allowlist` - run only allowlisted commands without asking.
- `ask` - use allowlist policy and ask on misses.
- `auto` - use allowlist policy, run deterministic matches directly, and send approval misses through OpenClaw's native auto reviewer before falling back to a human approval route.
- `full` - run host exec without approval prompts.

Legacy `tools.exec.security` / `tools.exec.ask` remain supported and still win
when set at the narrower session or agent scope.

### `exec.security`

<ParamField path="security" type='"deny" | "allowlist" | "full"'>
  - `deny` - block all host exec requests.
  - `allowlist` - allow only allowlisted commands.
  - `full` - allow everything (equivalent to elevated).

</ParamField>

### `exec.ask`

<ParamField path="ask" type='"off" | "on-miss" | "always"'>
  Configured ask policy for host exec. Controls the baseline approval
  prompt behavior from `tools.exec.ask` and host approvals defaults. The
  per-call `ask` tool parameter (see [Exec tool](/tools/exec#parameters))
  can only harden that baseline, and channel-origin model calls ignore it
  when the effective host ask is `off`.

- `off` - never prompt.
- `on-miss` - prompt only when the allowlist does not match.
- `always` - prompt on every command. `allow-always` durable trust does **not** suppress prompts when effective ask mode is `always`.

</ParamField>

### `askFallback`

<ParamField path="askFallback" type='"deny" | "allowlist" | "full"'>
  Resolution when a prompt is required but no UI is reachable. If this
  field is omitted, OpenClaw defaults to `deny`.

- `deny` - block.
- `allowlist` - allow only if allowlist matches.
- `full` - allow.

</ParamField>

### `tools.exec.strictInlineEval`

<ParamField path="strictInlineEval" type="boolean">
  When `true`, OpenClaw treats inline code-eval forms as approval-only
  even if the interpreter binary itself is allowlisted. Defense-in-depth
  for interpreter loaders that do not map cleanly to one stable file
  operand.
</ParamField>

Examples that strict mode catches:

- `python -c`
- `node -e`, `node --eval`, `node -p`
- `ruby -e`
- `perl -e`, `perl -E`
- `php -r`
- `lua -e`
- `osascript -e`

In strict mode these commands still need explicit approval, and
`allow-always` does not persist new allowlist entries for them
automatically.

### `tools.exec.commandHighlighting`

<ParamField path="commandHighlighting" type="boolean" default="false">
  Controls only presentation in exec approval prompts. When enabled,
  OpenClaw may attach parser-derived command spans so Web approval
  prompts can highlight command tokens. Set it to `true` to enable
  command text highlighting.
</ParamField>

This setting does **not** change `security`, `ask`, allowlist matching,
strict inline-eval behavior, approval forwarding, or command execution.
It can be set globally under `tools.exec.commandHighlighting` or per
agent under `agents.list[].tools.exec.commandHighlighting`.

## YOLO mode (no-approval)

If you want host exec to run without approval prompts, you must open
**both** policy layers - requested exec policy in OpenClaw config
(`tools.exec.*`) **and** host-local approvals policy in
the execution host approvals file.

OpenClaw defaults omitted `askFallback` to `deny`. Set host
`askFallback` to `full` explicitly when a no-UI approval prompt should
fall back to allow.

| Layer                 | YOLO setting               |
| --------------------- | -------------------------- |
| `tools.exec.security` | `full` on `gateway`/`node` |
| `tools.exec.ask`      | `off`                      |
| Host `askFallback`    | `full`                     |

<Warning>
**Important distinctions:**

- `tools.exec.host=auto` chooses **where** exec runs: sandbox when available, otherwise gateway.
- YOLO chooses **how** host exec is approved: `security=full` plus `ask=off`.
- In YOLO mode, OpenClaw does **not** add a separate heuristic command-obfuscation approval gate or script-preflight rejection layer on top of the configured host exec policy.
- `auto` does not make gateway routing a free override from a sandboxed session. A per-call `host=node` request is allowed from `auto`; `host=gateway` is only allowed from `auto` when no sandbox runtime is active. For a stable non-auto default, set `tools.exec.host` or use `/exec host=...` explicitly.

</Warning>

CLI-backed providers that expose their own noninteractive permission mode
can follow this policy. Claude CLI adds
`--permission-mode bypassPermissions` when OpenClaw's effective exec
policy is YOLO. For OpenClaw-managed Claude live sessions, OpenClaw's
effective exec policy is authoritative over Claude's native permission mode:
YOLO normalizes live launches to `--permission-mode bypassPermissions`, and
restrictive effective exec policy normalizes live launches to
`--permission-mode default`, even if raw Claude backend args specify another
mode.

If you want a more conservative setup, tighten OpenClaw exec policy back to
`allowlist` / `on-miss` or `deny`.

### Persistent gateway-host "never prompt" setup

<Steps>
  <Step title="Set the requested config policy">
    ```bash
    openclaw config set tools.exec.host gateway
    openclaw config set tools.exec.security full
    openclaw config set tools.exec.ask off
    openclaw gateway restart
    ```
  </Step>
  <Step title="Match the host approvals file">
    ```bash
    openclaw approvals set --stdin <<'EOF'
    {
      version: 1,
      defaults: {
        security: "full",
        ask: "off",
        askFallback: "full"
      }
    }
    EOF
    ```
  </Step>
</Steps>

### Local shortcut

```bash
openclaw exec-policy preset yolo
```

That local shortcut updates both:

- Local `tools.exec.host/security/ask`.
- Local approvals file defaults, including `askFallback: "full"`.

It is intentionally local-only. To change gateway-host or node-host
approvals remotely, use `openclaw approvals set --gateway` or
`openclaw approvals set --node <id|name|ip>`.

### Node host

For a node host, apply the same approvals file on that node instead:

```bash
openclaw approvals set --node <id|name|ip> --stdin <<'EOF'
{
  version: 1,
  defaults: {
    security: "full",
    ask: "off",
    askFallback: "full"
  }
}
EOF
```

<Note>
**Local-only limitations:**

- `openclaw exec-policy` does not synchronize node approvals.
- `openclaw exec-policy set --host node` is rejected.
- Node exec approvals are fetched from the node at runtime, so node-targeted updates must use `openclaw approvals --node ...`.

</Note>

### Session-only shortcut

- `/exec security=full ask=off` changes only the current session.
- `/elevated full` is a break-glass shortcut that skips exec approvals only when
  both the requested policy and the host approvals file resolve to
  `security: "full"` and `ask: "off"`. A stricter host file, such as
  `ask: "always"`, still prompts.

If the host approvals file stays stricter than config, the stricter host
policy still wins.

## Allowlist (per agent)

Allowlists are **per agent**. If multiple agents exist, switch which agent
you are editing in the macOS app. Patterns are glob matches.

Patterns can be resolved binary path globs or bare command-name globs.
Bare names match only commands invoked through `PATH`, so `rg` can match
`/opt/homebrew/bin/rg` when the command is `rg`, but **not** `./rg` or
`/tmp/rg`. Use a path glob when you want to trust one specific binary
location.

Legacy `agents.default` entries are migrated to `agents.main` on load.
Shell chains such as `echo ok && pwd` still need every top-level segment
to satisfy allowlist rules.

Examples:

- `rg`
- `~/Projects/**/bin/peekaboo`
- `~/.local/bin/*`
- `/opt/homebrew/bin/rg`

### Restricting arguments with argPattern

Add `argPattern` when an allowlist entry should match a binary and a
specific argument shape. OpenClaw evaluates the regular expression
against the parsed command arguments, excluding the executable token
(`argv[0]`). For hand-authored entries, arguments are joined with a
single space, so anchor the pattern when you need an exact match.

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "agents": {
    "main": {
      "allowlist": [
        {
          "pattern": "python3",
          "argPattern": "^safe\\.py$"
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

That entry allows `python3 safe.py`; `python3 other.py` is an allowlist
miss. If a path-only entry for the same binary is also present, unmatched
arguments can still fall back to that path-only entry. Omit the path-only
entry when the goal is to restrict the binary to the declared arguments.

Entries saved by approval flows can use an internal separator format for
exact argv matching. Prefer the UI or approval flow to regenerate those
entries instead of hand-editing the encoded value. If OpenClaw cannot
parse argv for a command segment, entries with `argPattern` do not match.

Each allowlist entry supports:

| Field              | Meaning                                                       |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `pattern`          | Resolved binary path glob or bare command-name glob           |
| `argPattern`       | Optional argv regex; omitted entries are path-only            |
| `id`               | Stable UUID used for UI identity                              |
| `source`           | Entry source, such as `allow-always`                          |
| `commandText`      | Command text captured when an approval flow created the entry |
| `lastUsedAt`       | Last-used timestamp                                           |
| `lastUsedCommand`  | Last command that matched                                     |
| `lastResolvedPath` | Last resolved binary path                                     |

## Auto-allow skill CLIs

When **Auto-allow skill CLIs** is enabled, executables referenced by
known skills are treated as allowlisted on nodes (macOS node or headless
node host). This uses `skills.bins` over the Gateway RPC to fetch the
skill bin list. Disable this if you want strict manual allowlists.

<Warning>
- This is an **implicit convenience allowlist**, separate from manual path allowlist entries.
- It is intended for trusted operator environments where Gateway and node are in the same trust boundary.
- If you require strict explicit trust, keep `autoAllowSkills: false` and use manual path allowlist entries only.

</Warning>

## Safe bins and approval forwarding

For safe bins (the stdin-only fast-path), interpreter binding details, and
how to forward approval prompts to Slack/Discord/Telegram (or run them as
native approval clients), see
[Exec approvals - advanced](/tools/exec-approvals-advanced).

## Control UI editing

Use the **Control UI → Nodes → Exec approvals** card to edit defaults,
per-agent overrides, and allowlists. Pick a scope (Defaults or an agent),
tweak the policy, add/remove allowlist patterns, then **Save**. The UI
shows last-used metadata per pattern so you can keep the list tidy.

The target selector chooses **Gateway** (local approvals) or a **Node**.
Nodes must advertise `system.execApprovals.get/set` (macOS app or
headless node host). If a node does not advertise exec approvals yet,
edit its local approvals file directly.

CLI: `openclaw approvals` supports gateway or node editing - see
[Approvals CLI](/cli/approvals).

## Approval flow

When a prompt is required, the gateway broadcasts
`exec.approval.requested` to operator clients. The Control UI and macOS
app resolve it via `exec.approval.resolve`, then the gateway forwards the
approved request to the node host.

For `host=node`, approval requests include a canonical `systemRunPlan`
payload. The gateway uses that plan as the authoritative
command/cwd/session context when forwarding approved `system.run`
requests.

That matters for async approval latency:

- The node exec path prepares one canonical plan up front.
- The approval record stores that plan and its binding metadata.
- Once approved, the final forwarded `system.run` call reuses the stored plan instead of trusting later caller edits.
- If the caller changes `command`, `rawCommand`, `cwd`, `agentId`, or `sessionKey` after the approval request was created, the gateway rejects the forwarded run as an approval mismatch.

## System events

Exec lifecycle is surfaced as system messages:

- `Exec running` (only if the command exceeds the running notice threshold).
- `Exec finished`.

These are posted to the agent's session after the node reports the event.
Denied exec approvals are terminal for the host command itself: the command
does not run. For main-agent async approvals with an originating session,
OpenClaw posts the denial back into that session as an internal followup so the
agent can stop waiting on the async command and avoid a missing-result repair.
If there is no session or the session cannot be resumed, OpenClaw can still
report a concise denial to the operator or direct chat route. Denials for
subagent sessions are not posted back into the subagent.
Gateway-host exec approvals emit the same lifecycle events when the
command finishes (and optionally when running longer than the threshold).
Approval-gated execs reuse the approval id as the `runId` in these
messages for easy correlation.

## Denied approval behavior

When an async exec approval is denied, OpenClaw treats the host command as
terminal and fail-closed. For main-agent sessions, the denial is delivered as an
internal session followup that tells the agent the async command did not run.
That preserves transcript continuity without exposing stale command output. If
session delivery is unavailable, OpenClaw falls back to a concise operator or
direct-chat denial when a safe route exists.

## Implications

- **`full`** is powerful; prefer allowlists when possible.
- **`ask`** keeps you in the loop while still allowing fast approvals.
- Per-agent allowlists prevent one agent's approvals from leaking into others.
- Approvals only apply to host exec requests from **authorized senders**. Unauthorized senders cannot issue `/exec`.
- `/exec security=full` is a session-level convenience for authorized operators and skips approvals by design. To hard-block host exec, set approvals security to `deny` or deny the `exec` tool via tool policy.

## Related

<CardGroup cols={2}>
  <Card title="Exec approvals - advanced" href="/tools/exec-approvals-advanced" icon="gear">
    Safe bins, interpreter binding, and approval forwarding to chat.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Exec tool" href="/tools/exec" icon="terminal">
    Shell command execution tool.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Elevated mode" href="/tools/elevated" icon="shield-exclamation">
    Break-glass path that also skips approvals.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Sandboxing" href="/gateway/sandboxing" icon="box">
    Sandbox modes and workspace access.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Security" href="/gateway/security" icon="lock">
    Security model and hardening.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Sandbox vs tool policy vs elevated" href="/gateway/sandbox-vs-tool-policy-vs-elevated" icon="sliders">
    When to reach for each control.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Skills" href="/tools/skills" icon="sparkles">
    Skill-backed auto-allow behavior.
  </Card>
</CardGroup>

target.title: feat(msteams): Microsoft Teams voice (CVI) + video + chat + governance
target.body:
## Summary Adds **Microsoft Teams as a first-class voice, video, and chat channel** for OpenClaw via the `msteams` provider and a full Conversational Video Interface (CVI). The agent answers and places Teams calls, *sees* the caller's camera and screen-share, *converses* in real time, *appears* as a lip-synced animated avatar, participates in group meetings (speaking only when addressed), and handles Teams chat — all with recording-gated, allowlisted, HMAC-authenticated transport. Teams audio/video cannot reach OpenClaw's existing webhook/media plane: Teams calls are delivered through Graph Calling signalling + the `Microsoft.Skype.Bots.Media` socket, which only run on .NET/Windows. This PR adds the OpenClaw-side orchestrator and the additive bridge protocol; a **separate companion media worker** (its own repo) owns the Graph/Skype.Bots.Media socket and renders the avatar tile. The three CVI pillars map directly onto what lands here: | Pillar | Meaning | In this PR | | --- | --- | --- | | **Perception** | the agent *sees* | inbound `video.frame` vision — `look_at_screen` + streaming auto-attach + realtime continuous vision (`sendImage`); camera + screen-share (VBSS); per-participant attribution in meetings | | **Dialogue** | the agent *converses* | realtime speech-to-speech (+ streaming STT→agent→TTS), barge-in, deterministic verbal interrupts, group "speak only when addressed" gate, recording-gated | | **Rendering** | the agent *is seen* | a lip-synced avatar tile driven by this PR's **expression cues**, **viseme `speech.marks`**, and **`show_to_caller`/`display.image`** senders (rendered by the companion worker) | ## What's included **Voice** - `msteams` provider on both voice-call modes — **streaming** (STT → agent → TTS) and **realtime** (speech-to-speech), with barge-in. - Caller **allowlist**, closed by default; recording-status gate before any media-derived data is processed. - **Realtime delegation** — the realtime model handles small talk and delegates real work to the OpenClaw agent: `openclaw_agent_consult` for inline answers (a "working on it" filler covers longer runs) and, under `realtime.toolPolicy: "owner"`, `openclaw_agent_task` for background jobs. - **Deterministic verbal interrupts** — "stop" / "wait" / "hold on" / "never mind" handled in code (streaming cuts playback and suppresses the reply; realtime flushes the worker queue), with wake-phrase-form and filler stripping, whole-utterance matching, and Unicode-aware Arabic support. - **DTMF / IVR** — in-band keypad tones surfaced to the realtime model so the agent can run "press 1 to…" flows (gated until recording is active). - **Bilingual Arabic/English** (opt-in) — detect, mirror, switch-with-caller, translate on request; the deterministic layers (interrupts, visemes) understand Arabic regardless. - **Roster greeting** — greets the caller by first name from the Teams roster and addresses participants by per-utterance speaker label. **Inbound video vision** - `video.frame` ingestion (buffered per source); `look_at_screen` tool (realtime) + automatic per-turn frame attachment (streaming); the agent sees the caller's **camera** and **screen-share**. - **Realtime continuous vision** — `RealtimeVoiceBridge.sendImage` (a small core plugin-sdk addition + openai/azure realtime provider) pushes the latest changed frame as an ambient `input_image` item every ~6 s, no forced response, budget-capped and recording-gated. - **Scene-change-driven ambient vision** (camera + screen simultaneously, per-source dedup + labels) and **retroactive vision** — a ring of 16 scene-change keyframes per call; `look_at_screen scope:"history"` attaches up to 6 timestamped, attributed keyframes to one budgeted consult. - Per-call **vision spend cap** (`maxVisionPerMinute`, default 30, `0` = unlimited) across all consumers. **CVI rendering drivers** (sent by this PR; rendered by the companion worker; additive — older workers ignore unknown fields) - **Expression cues** — a dependency-free lexical heuristic infers neutral/happy/sad/surprised from reply text and emits an `expression` message; a "thinking" expression holds while a tool runs. - **Viseme lip-sync** — `speech.marks` carry a per-sound timeline so the avatar mouth changes shape; the full pass uses real ElevenLabs `/with-timestamps` character alignment (falls back to a text estimator when absent; audio never depends on timing). - **Visual sharing** — `show_to_caller` runs the agent to produce one trusted-local image (remote URLs excluded — no SSRF) and renders it via `display.image`, either fullscreen or as a **picture-in-picture overlay** over the avatar, with caption strips and paced multi-image slideshows. **Group / meeting calls** - **Per-participant attribution** — each frame carries `participantId` / `participantName`; the streaming path labels the attached image and `look_at_screen` names the participant, so the model can reason about who is showing what. Active-speaker camera follow / re-select. - **Speak only when addressed** — in a group call (2+ humans) the assistant stays silent until addressed by name (`wakePhrases`), with a short follow-up window; 1:1 always responds. Deterministic on streaming (`shouldRespondToGroupTurn`, evaluated at the audio egress on realtime too); time-windowed and partial-wake aware. **Outbound (call me back)** - `initiateCall` places a Teams call via the authenticated, SSRF-guarded endpoint; the agent speaks the **result**, then hangs up. Delivery via Teams **chat message** (default) or **voice call-back**. - **Greet-on-answer** — the call-back speaks only once the callee answers (recording-active), not while the phone is still ringing. - Notify mode delivers the result and hangs up drain-aware (no clipping); voicemail fallback leaves the same result when unanswered; no-answer timeout finalizes calls that never connect. **Chat & governance** - **"Ask OpenClaw about this" message action** — a Teams message-context command quotes the selected chat message into a prompt and dispatches it through the normal message path (requires the app manifest to declare the `askOpenClaw` command). - **Voice-message transcription** (opt-in) — inbound Teams audio clips are transcribed and folded into the agent's turn; a clip that fails keeps its attachment placeholder. - **Audit-log channel** (opt-in) — agent replies are mirrored to a named Teams conversation at the delivery choke point (so natively-streamed replies are in the trail too); a loop guard keeps the audit channel's own traffic out. - **DLP outbound redaction** (opt-in) — built-in categories plus custom patterns redacted on every outbound surface (block messages, streamed replies, edits, adaptive cards, poll cards). Because per-token streaming can't retract a secret completing across chunks, DLP downgrades `streaming.mode: "partial"` to `"progress"`. **Meeting productivity** - **End-of-meeting recap** (opt-in) — key points / decisions / action items posted to the caller's Teams chat (1:1) or into the meeting thread (group) at call end. - **On-demand minutes** mid-call (`post_meeting_minutes`), a real **OOXML `.docx` minutes artifact** (built deterministically in code via `jszip`), and **per-person attribution** from unmixed meeting audio ("Sara: …" instead of one mixed stream) rendered into the document. **Sessions & memory** - Caller identity (Teams AAD object id, else a per-call-unique id) keys the session; `sessionScope: "per-thread"` keys by Teams meeting/chat thread so parallel conversations stay isolated. PSTN falls through to per-phone. ## Architecture OpenClaw runs the orchestration in this PR — the `msteams` voice-call provider, the HMAC-authenticated WebSocket bridge, STT/agent/TTS and realtime delegation, the group/vision/governance logic, and all CVI **drivers** (`expression`, `speech.marks`, `display.image`, `sendImage`). A **separate companion Windows/.NET media worker** (its own repo) owns the Graph Calling signalling and the `Microsoft.Skype.Bots.Media` socket, and renders the avatar tile (RMS lip-sync blended with viseme shapes, head bob/sway, blink, gaze, expression-shaped mouths, and the `display.image` overlay). It pairs with this provider over the versioned, additive bridge protocol. The worker is intentionally **out of scope here** — this PR adds no Teams/Graph SDK and no new runtime dependencies (reuses `ws` / `zod` / plugin-sdk), so msteams logic can be bundled in `voice-call` or lifted into a separate optional plugin per maintainer preference. ## Recent reliability fixes - **Echo self-answer fix** — `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` now defaults **on** (default-off was a live self-answer bug); the echo guard runs on a playout clock, and the opening greeting allows no barge-in until the caller's first real turn, so a speakerphone echo can't make the bot re-greet in a loop. - **Group-meeting wake gate** — time-windowed, partial-wake; an unaddressed meeting turn's reply audio is dropped deterministically at the egress on the realtime path (not model-trusted). - **Meeting-minutes recipient** — recap/minutes are target-locked to the caller's chat (1:1) or the meeting thread (group), gated behind the opt-in so the bot never posts unprompted. - **Screen-share** — `look_at_screen` offered under a safe read-only posture with browser auto-start guidance; scene-change detection so switching shared content is picked up, not stuck on the previous page. - **Replay-proof handshake** — single-use `(callId, timestamp, signature)` HMAC tuples expiring at `ts+window` (clock-skew safe); legitimate reconnects carry a fresh timestamp. - **Teardown** — a single `releaseCallState` owner every path funnels through (no stranded "un-hangup-able" calls, no leaked vision/recording state, declined outbound calls release their CallRecord). ## Behavior changes Defaults preserve prior behavior except two flagged changes: - `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` flipped **off → on** (fixes the self-answer echo bug; set `false` to restore, tune via `echoSuppressionWindowMs` / `echoBargeInRms`). - Outbound call-backs honor `outbound.defaultMode` (default **notify**: state the result, then hang up after audio drains) and **speak only on answer** (set `"conversation"` to keep the lingering behavior). All other additions are opt-in: `msteams.meetingRecap`, `msteams.bilingual`, `channels.msteams.transcribeVoiceMessages`, `channels.msteams.auditChannel`, `channels.msteams.dlp.enabled`. ## Config (example) ```jsonc "voice-call": { "config": { "msteams": { "port": 8443, "path": "/voice/msteams/stream", "sharedSecret": "", "requireRecordingStatus": true, "bindAddress": "127.0.0.1", "maxVisionPerMinute": 30, "outbound": { "enabled": true, "workerBaseUrl": "", "answerTimeoutMs": 120000 }, "groupCall": { "requireAddress": true, "wakePhrases": ["assistant", ""], "followUpWindowMs": 12000 } }, "realtime": { "enabled": true } } } ``` `tenantId` is read from `channels.msteams.tenantId`. `groupCall` only affects calls with 2+ humans; 1:1 always responds. `maxVisionPerMinute` caps per-call vision spend across `look_at_screen`, streaming auto-attach, and the realtime ambient push. Audio is **PCM 16 kHz**. ### Security - WS server binds **loopback by default** (`bindAddress` for a trusted interface); HMAC-SHA256 over `timestamp + callId`, constant-time compare; replay window; 64 KB inbound payload cap; per-IP / total / pending connection caps; strict `zod` discriminated-union message validation; secret as `SecretInput`/SecretRef, never logged; SSRF-guarded outbound place-call. Lifecycle-managed: the server starts as an awaited init step (bind failures surface) and stops on teardown. ## Relationship to other PRs Coexists with the foundation PR **#91438** (Microsoft Teams CVI voice/video provider); this PR is not stacked on it. ## Testing / proof Each feature ships unit tests — provider lifecycle, WS transport (HMAC accept/reject, replay 401/reconnect, payload-cap close `1009`), the pure group-call gate, interrupt matcher (positive/negative, wake-phrase, Arabic), viseme/display paths, history ring, group-gate egress drop, recap prompt, DTMF mapping, message-action extraction, teardown/leak regressions, and pre-recording transcript drop. Core flows were verified end-to-end on **real Microsoft Teams calls** (production M365 tenant; gateway from source; companion media worker as a Windows service; Azure OpenAI realtime; ElevenLabs STT/TTS), across 1:1 and group calls, voice and CVI: - **Voice / realtime** — multi-turn live ASR→agent→TTS round-trips; roster greeting by name; no echo self-answer on open speakers; barge-in; deterministic interrupts in English, wake-phrase form, and Arabic ("توقف"); bilingual mirroring + on-request translation. - **Vision / CVI** — `look_at_screen` answered across multiple screen-share windows (incl. content switches and "what did the first screen say"); the avatar published a live video tile; the "thinking" expression held across tool runs; `show_to_caller` rendered captioned PiP slideshows with the coded hold times; real ElevenLabs `(aligned)` viseme timing observed. - **Outbound** — call-back placed → Established → recording-active gate → result spoken only after answer → notify-mode auto-hangup after drain → clean teardown, no leaked state; voicemail fallback delivered the same result when unanswered. - **Meeting / chat / governance** — on-hangup recap + minutes landed in the Teams chat; background task delivered its answer to chat; audit mirror produced one line per streamed DM reply with no self-mirror inside the audit channel; a DLP-redacted email rendered as `[REDACTED:email]` on the streamed wire path; the "Ask OpenClaw about this" message action dispatched and replied. Not live-tested in this tenant (unit-tested instead): voice-message transcription (the tenant exposes no bot-deliverable voice clip; the graceful no-media path was observed), the DLP `secret` category on the wire (the model refused to repeat a dummy key; the email category exercises the same path), and the group-chat form of the message action. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Microsoft Teams voice/video/CVI + chat as a first-class OpenClaw channel — the `msteams` voice provider answers/places real Teams calls, sees the caller's screen-share/camera, renders a lip-synced avatar, and runs chat governance (DLP / audit / minutes). - **Real environment tested:** Production Microsoft 365 tenant; OpenClaw gateway built from source; the companion Windows/.NET media worker running as a Windows service; Azure OpenAI realtime; ElevenLabs STT/TTS. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Enabled `voice-call` `provider: "msteams"` + `realtime` and the `channels.msteams` governance blocks in `openclaw.json`, started the gateway (`openclaw gateway run --force`), then placed real Teams 1:1 and group calls (voice + screen-share), an outbound "call me back", and a chat-governance session. - **Evidence after fix (screenshots, recording, redacted logs):** Captured from real Microsoft Teams calls against a production tenant (redacted): **1. Be-seen avatar — the bot publishes its avatar as a live video tile** **2. `show_to_caller` — the bot screenshots a page and displays it fullscreen on its own video tile mid-call** **3. Outbound "call me back" — the bot rings the caller on Teams** **4. Result delivery even to voicemail — Teams transcribes the call-back's spoken result** **5. Screen recording of a live call** https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a961d8ce-22f8-44a9-ae28-b475393e3115 Plus the redacted gateway/worker logs + transcripts in the **Proof & live-test evidence** section below (voice ASR→agent→TTS round-trip, realtime session lifecycle, the three-call recording-gate refuse→open, outbound notify call-back lifecycle, background-task→Teams-chat delivery reading the real machine clock, audit `🧾` mirror, and DLP `[REDACTED:email]` on the streamed wire path). - **Observed result after fix:** Multi-turn round-trips with audible replies; `look_at_screen` answered across two screen-share windows incl. a content switch + retroactive history; the avatar published a live video tile; the outbound call-back spoke the result only after answer then auto-hung-up cleanly with no leaked state; the on-hangup recap + minutes landed in the caller's Teams chat as a real `.docx`; DLP redacted a secret on the live streamed reply. - **What was not tested:** Voice-message transcription (the tenant exposed no bot-deliverable voice clip; the graceful no-media path was observed), the DLP `secret` category on the wire (the model refused to repeat a dummy key; the `email` category exercises the same path), and the group-chat form of the message action — each is unit-tested instead. - **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Private tenant identifiers (AAD object ids, call ids, endpoints) are redacted, and some evidence is redacted runtime logs rather than recordings; the avatar is rendered by the companion media worker (separate repo), so its screenshot is the viewable artifact for the CVI rendering drivers this PR emits. - **Before evidence (optional but encouraged):** The pre-fix `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` default-off self-answer bug was reproduced before the fix (a speakerphone echo made the bot re-greet itself in a loop); fixed by defaulting it on plus the playout-clock echo guard. ## Proof & live-test evidence All evidence below is from real Microsoft Teams calls against a production tenant (private identifiers redacted). ![OpenClaw companion worker rendering the animated "jarvis" avatar live on the bot's Teams video tile during a production-tenant validation call](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/06af3f1f-b943-4a40-88c9-cd4eb95fc7ba) *Avatar shown is the worker-side `AvatarImagePath=jarvis` Lottie loop with speech-reactive zoom (consuming this PR's `expression` / `speech.marks` viseme / `display.image` rendering drivers); the default `AvatarImagePath=avatar` SVG style adds full lip-sync viseme mouth shapes, expressions, and blink.* ### Voice / realtime **Full inbound audio round-trip on a real multi-turn Teams call** — caller speech → ElevenLabs STT → agent (`microsoft-foundry/gpt-5.4`) → ElevenLabs TTS, across multiple turns (~70 s). Disfluencies in the transcript confirm live ASR; `spoken` payloads are exactly what was synthesized back to the caller. ``` user (STT): "How are you, man?" asst (spoken): "I'm doing well, man - thanks. What can I help you with?" user (STT): "Tell me more about you." asst (spoken): "I'm your phone assistant in OpenClaw - calm, practical, here to help..." ```
Gateway lifecycle log (streaming path, provider msteams) ``` MsteamsMediaStream listening port=8443 path=/voice/msteams/stream [voice-call] msteams realtime transcription provider: elevenlabs [voice-call] msteams TTS provider configured MsteamsMediaStream: connection open MsteamsProvider: session.start callId= threadId= caller.aadId= MsteamsMediaStream: connection closed ```
**Speech-to-speech realtime mode on a real call** — operator placed a Teams call, spoke several turns, and heard the OpenAI-realtime bot reply; clean session lifecycle, 0 errors. **Verbal interrupts / barge-in on the ~7.5 min inbound 1:1 validation call** — deterministic verbal interrupts in English, by wake-phrase, and in Arabic ("توقف"); bilingual mirroring + translation; consult with the "thinking" avatar expression across six tool runs. ### Vision & CVI **`look_at_screen` and `show_to_caller` on the same validation call** — `look_at_screen` handled a scene change plus retroactive keyframe history across two screen-share windows; `show_to_caller` rendered a captioned PiP slideshow at exactly the coded hold times (4500 ms non-final / 5000 ms final), verified in the worker log. The avatar screenshot above is the live consumer of the `expression` / `speech.marks` viseme / `display.image` drivers this PR emits. ### Outbound call-back **Outbound "call me when it's done" notify call-back on a real call**, placed by an in-call background task: `placed → Established → recording-gate open → result spoken only after answer → notify-mode auto-hangup after audio drain` — complete, un-clipped delivery with clean teardown. **`openclaw_agent_task` background task → Teams chat delivery on a real call.** Caller said *"take care of local time in the background and ping me on Teams."* The realtime model invoked `openclaw_agent_task`, spoke the ack, the caller hung up, the agent ran in the background, and the result landed in the caller's Teams chat — reading the real machine clock, not a canned reply: ``` > I'm checking the local machine time now and will send it here shortly. > Local machine time: 2026-06-04 05:28:13 +00:00. ``` ### Meeting / chat / governance **Recording-status gate (Microsoft Media Access API) observed refusing, then opening, across real calls.** With `requireRecordingStatus: true` (default), delegation is refused until the external worker signals recording active (the caller audibly hears a "recording isn't active" reply), then the gate opens and `openclaw_agent_consult` runs.
Redacted gateway log across real calls (recording gate) ``` # Call A — no recording signal → consult refused MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=0c005180… caller.aadId= MsteamsMediaStream: connection closed 0c005180… # Call B — worker reports recording active → gate opens → consult runs MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=2d005480… caller.aadId= MsteamsProvider: recording.status 2d005480… = active (realtime) <-- gate opens ```
**Governance on the wire** — the audit-channel mirror produced a `🧾` line per streamed DM reply (loop guard holding for the bot's own reply inside the audit chat), and DLP rendered `[REDACTED:email]` in the Teams client on the **streamed** path. On-hangup recap plus on-demand minutes both landed in chat. ### Message action — "Ask OpenClaw about this" The Teams message-context command quotes the selected chat message into a prompt and dispatches it through the normal message path; on the 2026-06-12 governance session it **dispatched and replied** in the Teams client. The invoke-dispatch path was merged with upstream's adaptive-card value helpers in `monitor-handler.ts` (both kept), with message-action extraction unit-covered. (The group-chat form is unit-tested, not live-tested in this tenant.) ### Setup / upgrade compatibility (fresh-start & upgrade path) **Everything here is additive and backward-compatible — no data-model migration is required** (this is the upgrade-path evidence for the compatibility / "serialized state" flags): - **All new behavior is OPT-IN config.** The `msteams` voice provider and every governance feature are off unless explicitly configured: `msteams.meetingRecap`, `msteams.bilingual`, `channels.msteams.transcribeVoiceMessages`, `channels.msteams.auditChannel`, `channels.msteams.dlp.enabled`, plus the `msteams.groupCall` / `msteams.outbound` blocks. A config that doesn't set these behaves exactly as before. - **Existing voice-call providers are unaffected.** `twilio` / `telnyx` / `plivo` / `mock` route and behave unchanged; `msteams` is a new enum case resolved only when selected, on its own authenticated bridge listener (`msteams.port`/`path`), not the public webhook plane. - **plugin-sdk additions are optional with a no-op facade.** `RealtimeVoiceImageInput` / `sendImage` and the TTS alignment surface are optional contract additions; realtime providers that don't implement them get a no-op facade — no behavior change for non-Teams voice. - **No persisted-state migration.** The files flagged as "serialized state" are **test fixtures** (`elevenlabs/*.test.ts`, `openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts`) and **in-memory per-call state** (`msteams-realtime.ts`, `session-runtime.ts`); nothing persisted crosses versions. `bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts` is **regenerated tooling output** (the validation schema for the new opt-in keys), not user data — old configs validate as before. - **Two flagged default changes only**, both fixing live-reproduced bugs and both reversible: `realtime.suppressInputDuringPlayback` off → on (self-answer echo fix; set `false` to restore), and outbound `defaultMode` defaults to `notify` + speak-only-on-answer (set `"conversation"` to restore). **New real `.docx` minutes.** End-of-meeting minutes are now a real OOXML Word document with **deterministic per-speaker attribution** ("Sara: …") from unmixed meeting audio, delivered to chat alongside the on-hangup recap. Unit coverage: the voice-call suite is **610 passing**, with dedicated suites for the docx minutes, welcome-card DLP, recording gate, interrupt matcher (incl. Arabic), and DLP cards. ## Acknowledgements Contributed by the team at the **Ports, Customs and Free Zone Corporation (PCFC)** — Dubai, United Arab Emirates — bringing Microsoft Teams voice & video (the Conversational Video Interface) to OpenClaw for public-sector use. We welcome the maintainers' guidance on shaping it for the wider community.

target.title: fix(telegram): handle EADDRNOTAVAIL local socket exhaustion correctly [AI-assisted]
target.body:
### Summary
This PR resolves an issue where the Telegram transport incorrectly attempts IP-rotation fallback retries when hitting a local socket allocation failure (`EADDRNOTAVAIL`).

### Changes
- Updated `shouldUseTelegramTransportFallback` in `extensions/telegram/src/fetch.ts` to detect `EADDRNOTAVAIL` error codes or socket allocation failure error messages.
- If `EADDRNOTAVAIL` is detected, it logs an accurate warning message ("telegram transport encountered local socket allocation failure (EADDRNOTAVAIL) — check ephemeral ports / mbuf zones / network extensions; remote IP rotation will not help") and returns `false` to immediately throw the error, skipping unnecessary IP-rotation retries.
- Added a unit test in `extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts` to verify the correct handling of `EADDRNOTAVAIL` socket allocation failures.

### Real behavior proof
All tests in `extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts` pass successfully:
```
 ✓  extension-telegram  ../../extensions/telegram/src/fetch.test.ts (43 tests) 2246ms
     ✓ normalizes a full bot endpoint apiRoot before callers append bot paths  305ms
```

target.title: fix(ports): route isPortBusy through checkPortInUse to catch IPv4-only occupants
target.body:
## Summary

Fix `isPortBusy` preflight to detect IPv4-only port occupants by routing through the existing multi-endpoint `checkPortInUse` probe instead of a single hostless `tryListenOnPort` bind.

**Problem**: `isPortBusy` in `src/cli/ports.ts` called `tryListenOnPort({ port, exclusive: true })` without specifying a host. On dual-stack hosts, this binds the IPv6 wildcard (`::`), which does not conflict with an occupant listening only on `127.0.0.1` (IPv4-only). The result: `isPortBusy` returns `false` (port appears free) when it is actually occupied.

**Impact**: This guards the `gateway --force` flow (`forceFreePortAndWait`). A false "not busy" means the force-kill path concludes there is nothing to reclaim while an IPv4-only listener is in fact occupying the port.

**Fix**: Route `isPortBusy` through `checkPortInUse` (newly exported from `src/infra/ports-inspect.ts`), which probes all four endpoints — `127.0.0.1`, `0.0.0.0`, `::1`, `::` — instead of a single bare listen. Map its `"busy"` result to `true`.

This is the same address-family invariant fixed for the CDP preflight in #94415.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/ports-inspect.ts:637` | Export `checkPortInUse` (was private `async function`) |
| `src/cli/ports.ts:6,132-137` | Replace `tryListenOnPort` import with `checkPortInUse`; rewrite `isPortBusy` |
| `src/cli/program.force.test.ts:234-241` | Update mock: `mockResolvedValueOnce` → `mockResolvedValue` |

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/cli/program.force.test.ts` — 17/17 tests pass
- Code flow: `isPortBusy` → `checkPortInUse` → probes `["127.0.0.1", "0.0.0.0", "::1", "::"]` via `tryListenOnHost`. An IPv4-only listener on `127.0.0.1` now correctly triggers `EADDRINUSE` → `"busy"` → `true`.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix for issue #94426: `isPortBusy` preflight misses IPv4-only occupants (same address-family flaw as #94379)

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1
- **Platform**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64 (dual-stack)

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```bash
cd $WORKTREE
pnpm test src/cli/program.force.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
 ✓ |cli| src/cli/program.force.test.ts (17 tests) 870ms
   ✓ gateway --force helpers (17)
     ✓ parses lsof output into pid/command pairs
     ✓ returns empty list when lsof finds nothing
     ✓ skips lsof when the port is already bindable
     ✓ throws when lsof missing
     ✓ kills each listener and returns metadata
     ✓ retries until the port is free
     ✓ escalates to SIGKILL if SIGTERM doesn't free the port
     ✓ bounds oversized force-free intervals by the remaining timeout
     ✓ falls back to fuser when lsof is permission denied
     ✓ uses fuser SIGKILL escalation when port stays busy
     ✓ throws when lsof is unavailable and fuser is missing

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `isPortBusy` now detects IPv4-only occupants by probing `127.0.0.1` first. On a dual-stack host, an IPv4-only listener triggers `EADDRINUSE` on the first host probe, and `checkPortInUse` returns `"busy"`.

**What was not tested**: Live dual-stack reproduction with a real IPv4-only listener (vitest mock correctly simulates per-host `EADDRINUSE`). Cross-platform behavior (Windows/macOS).

## Risk checklist

- **Backward compatibility**: ✅ No API changes. `isPortBusy` is private; `forceFreePortAndWait` public contract unchanged.
- **Performance**: `checkPortInUse` short-circuits on first busy host. In the "busy" case (common path), same cost. In the "free" case (final poll iteration), probes all 4 hosts (~4× latency, negligible in practice).
- **Error handling**: Conservative: only `"busy"` maps to `true`; `"unknown"`/`"skip"` treated as not-busy (let caller retry).
- **Dependencies**: None new. Reuses existing `checkPortInUse` from #94415.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?**
- Maintainer review

**What is still waiting?**
- CI pass on the PR
- Maintainer approval of the canonical approach (reuse `checkPortInUse`)

**Bot/reviewer comments addressed**:
- clawsweeper on #94426 suggested reusing `checkPortInUse` — this PR follows that recommendation
- Multiple existing PRs (#94456, #94471, #94448, #94463, #94469) propose different fixes; this PR implements the suggested canonical approach

## References

target.title: fix(dispatch): route durable reasoning payloads to Telegram
target.body:
## Summary

Under `/reasoning on`, Telegram users were not receiving thinking messages from reasoning models. The thinking content was correctly generated by the model and archived in the session, but the shared dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) unconditionally suppressed all `isReasoning` payloads before they could reach the Telegram-specific reasoning lane handler.

This PR routes durable `isReasoning` block and final payloads through to Telegram by skipping the generic suppression when the delivery channel is Telegram. Other channels (WhatsApp, web, etc.) continue to suppress reasoning payloads as before.

Fixes #94937

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `/reasoning on` was dropping all thinking messages on Telegram because the generic dispatch suppressed `isReasoning` payloads before Telegram's reasoning lane could process them.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node.js v24.16.0, OpenClaw main @ 2026-06-19, test suite run via vitest.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran the full dispatch-from-config test suite, the Telegram bot-message-dispatch tests, and the reasoning emit tests to verify the fix and confirm no regressions on other channels.
```bash
# Run the dispatch-from-config tests (covers both block and final reply paths)
npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts

# Run Telegram bot-message-dispatch tests (covers reasoning splitting/handling)
npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts

# Run reasoning emit tests
npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.emits-reasoning-as-separate-message-enabled.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**: All 355 tests pass with 0 failures. The two-line change correctly gates the `isReasoning` suppression on the delivery channel — only skipping it for `telegram`.
```
✓ src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts (210 tests)
✓ extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts (129 tests)
✓ src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.emits-reasoning-as-separate-message-enabled.test.ts (16 tests)
```

The change is at two points in `dispatch-from-config.ts`:
- **Block delivery** (line ~3107): `payload.isReasoning === true` suppression now only applies when `deliveryChannel !== "telegram"`
- **Final reply loop** (line ~3279): `reply.isReasoning === true` skip now only applies when `deliveryChannel !== "telegram"`

**Observed result after the fix**: `isReasoning` block and final payloads now reach Telegram delivery, where the existing reasoning lane coordinator (`bot-message-dispatch.ts` lines 290-292, 932-934, 1164-1166) splits thinking from answer and persists them as separate messages under `/reasoning on`.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end Telegram session with a reasoning model (DeepSeek/Claude) using `/reasoning on`. Source-level coverage is provided by the 355 existing tests across dispatch, Telegram dispatch, and reasoning emit paths.

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
# 210 passed

npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
# 129 passed

npx vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.emits-reasoning-as-separate-message-enabled.test.ts
# 16 passed
```

### Integration / E2E

The change is a 2-line gate addition. Reasoning suppression behavior is unchanged for all non-Telegram channels. Telegram's existing reasoning lane handling (streaming split, progress draft, step state) is unchanged — the fix only ensures the payloads actually reach it.

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — only affects Telegram; all other channels continue to suppress reasoning payloads exactly as before
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 355 related tests pass
- [x] I have updated relevant documentation — no API/config changes to document
- [x] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — N/A, no breaking changes

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: fix(workboard): filter archived cards in CLI list command
target.body:
## Summary

The `workboard_list` MCP tool and agent command filter out soft-archived cards by default. The CLI `workboard list` command did not apply the same filter, causing CLI/API inconsistency.

Add `.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt)` to the CLI list command to match the existing behavior in `command.ts` and `tools.ts`.

Fixes #94555

## What Problem This Solves

The `workboard list` CLI command displayed all cards including soft-archived cards (cards with `metadata.archivedAt` set). The `workboard_list` MCP tool (`tools.ts`) and the agent command (`command.ts`) already filter out archived cards by default using the same `.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt)` guard. Users saw inconsistent results between the CLI and MCP/agent interfaces, with archived cards cluttering the CLI output.

## Evidence

The CLI test suite passes with the added filter line. The guard is identical to the one already shipping in `command.ts` and `tools.ts`:

```bash
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

The filter pattern at `cli.ts:136`:
```typescript
cards = cards.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt);
```

matches the identical pattern in:
- `command.ts:97`: `cards.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt)`
- `tools.ts:257`: `cards.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt)`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: The `workboard list` CLI command displayed all cards including soft-archived ones (cards with `metadata.archivedAt` set). The `workboard_list` MCP tool (`tools.ts:257`) and the agent command (`command.ts:97`) already filter out archived cards by default using the same `.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt)` guard. This caused inconsistent behavior between CLI and MCP/agent interfaces.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x86_64
- **Test framework**: Vitest v4.1.8
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-94555-workboard-list-filter`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
cd /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-94555-workboard-list-filter
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

The added filter at `extensions/workboard/src/cli.ts:136`:
```typescript
// FIX #94555: Match the MCP tool / API behavior — exclude soft-archived
// cards by default unless explicitly opted in.
cards = cards.filter((card) => !card.metadata?.archivedAt);
```

is identical to the guard in `command.ts:97` and `tools.ts:257`.

**Observed result after the fix**: The CLI `workboard list` command now excludes soft-archived cards (those with `metadata.archivedAt` set), matching the behavior of the MCP `workboard_list` tool and the agent command. The fix is a single `.filter()` line that reuses the same guard pattern already present in two other code paths.

**What was not tested**: Live Workboard instance with real archived cards in a production environment. The filter logic is identical to two other shipping code paths (`command.ts` and `tools.ts`) that have been running in production without issues.

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `Yes` — archived cards are no longer shown in CLI `workboard list` output, matching the MCP/API behavior. Users who relied on the CLI showing archived cards will need to use the explicit board view.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No`.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`.

**What is the highest-risk area?** Users who previously used CLI to view archived cards will no longer see them.

**How is that risk mitigated?** The MCP tool and agent command already filter out archived cards, so the MCP/API users already have this behavior. CLI users can still view all cards through the board-specific views.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested.

target.title: fix(agent): surface non_deliverable_terminal_turn with specific message and auto-retry
target.body:
## Summary

When a model turn ends with `stop_reason: tool_use` but no explicit terminal delivery, the runtime classifies it as `non_deliverable_terminal_turn`. Previously, this `terminalError` was only recorded in trajectory metadata and never surfaced to the user. The user would see the generic "Something went wrong… use /new" message, which gives misleading remediation advice and hides the actual failure.

## Changes

1. **Propagate `trajectoryTerminalError`** from attempt trajectory through `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta` so the reply layer can see it
2. **Auto-retry once** for `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` — the model produced no output, so retry is idempotent and usually resolves the issue
3. **Surface a specific, actionable message** when retry is exhausted

## Files changed

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — add `trajectoryTerminalError` to return
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/types.ts` — add type for `trajectoryTerminalError`
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts` — add to `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta`
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` — propagate through meta
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-failure-copy.ts` — add user-facing message constant
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` — auto-retry + specific message
- `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` — 3 new tests

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` (stop_reason=tool_use, no delivery) surfaced as generic "Something went wrong… use /new" with no diagnostic signal and no retry. The fix propagates the terminal error to the reply layer, auto-retries once, and surfaces a specific actionable message on exhaustion.

Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw main branch (commit d1ab308f5c).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `pnpm build` — clean build, exit 0
2. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-trajectory-status` — 19/19 passed
3. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution` — 207/207 passed (204 existing + 3 new: auto-retry, exhausted retry message, no retry when fallback exhausted)
4. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier` — passed
5. `pnpm test src/trajectory/export` — passed
6. `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/` — 277/298 passed (21 failures pre-existing in unrelated auth/failover/setup)
7. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/` — 297/339 passed (agent-runner-execution.test.ts: 207/207)

Evidence after fix:
- New tests verify 3 code paths: (a) auto-retry triggers when trajectoryTerminalError === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn" with no visible payloads — embedded runner called twice, second succeeds; (b) exhausted retry returns kind: "final" with NON_DELIVERABLE_TERMINAL_TURN_USER_TEXT: "⚠️ The model stopped between tool calls without producing a reply. Please try the same message again — this usually resolves on retry. If it persists, use /new or a lighter model."; (c) fallback-exhausted path skips retry and still surfaces the specific message instead of generic text.
- trajectoryTerminalError field flows: resolveAttemptTrajectoryTerminal → attempt.ts return → run.ts meta → EmbeddedAgentRunMeta → agent-runner-execution.ts reply layer
- Auto-retry uses consumeNonDeliverableTurnRetry() with 1-retry budget (same pattern as consumeTransientHttpRetry)

Observed result after fix:
- All 207 agent-runner-execution tests pass with no regressions
- Type checker passes on changed files (pnpm tsgo)
- Build succeeds (pnpm build, exit 0)
- When trajectoryTerminalError === "non_deliverable_terminal_turn" with empty payloads, the code auto-retries once before surfacing the specific message

What was not tested: Full end-to-end reproduction with Telegram DM under heavy cacheRead load. Requires production-like environment with live API keys, sustained load, and a provider model to trigger the race between stop_reason=tool_use and turn closure. Unit tests cover the code paths; Crabbox E2E would be needed for live roundtrip proof.

target.title: fix(agents): verify delivery receipts before display
target.body:
## Summary

This fixes a user-visible trust bug in the agent reply pipeline: assistant text can no longer present a message delivery receipt as fact unless the current turn has matching evidence from a successful message tool send.

- Before: model text could say a message was accepted, queued, sent, or include a provider id even when no send result backed that claim.
- After: unsupported delivery receipt claims are replaced before visible delivery, while matching current-turn send evidence still allows the receipt through.
- Scope is intentionally message-delivery only. This PR does not add a generic plugin evidence API or claim support for tickets, payments, calendar events, email, or other side effects.
- Reviewer focus: detector scope, evidence matching, and the stream/block/final payload guard points.

[size/exempt: 23 files, +2272/-41 because the fix needs proof across streaming, block replies, chunked replies, retry/final payload delivery, and tool-result evidence paths. This is not a mechanical PR.]

## Linked context

Related review/proof discussion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/94693#issuecomment-4746612520

Current base/head used for local validation:

- Base: `origin/main` at `061a3705dbe28ddd058afb298976f09d8857b43b`
- Head: `7a9090a8373217979bc8e863bb8e1f7fa71e6a70`

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: user-visible agent replies could claim message delivery status or provider receipt ids without matching current-turn delivery evidence.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on this branch, using the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` bridge with real runtime events and no Vitest assertion runner.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `timeout -k5s 120s node --import tsx` with an inline script that imports `./src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.js`, sends actual `tool_execution_start`, `tool_execution_end`, and assistant `text_end` events, then asserts unsupported and verified visible replies.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
[proof-94693] unsupported claim visible reply: {"text":"I cannot verify that this SMS was sent. I do not have matching current-turn delivery evidence, so please check the messaging provider history or use the verified send flow before reporting it as sent.","audioAsVoice":false,"replyToTag":true,"replyToCurrent":true}
[proof-94693] verified delivery evidence: [{"channel":"sms","toolName":"message","providerId":"SM_proof_redacted_1234","status":"queued","sender":"+15557654321","recipient":"+15551234567"}]
[proof-94693] verified claim visible reply: {"text":"I sent the SMS. Status: accepted/queued. Message ID: SM_proof_redacted_1234","audioAsVoice":false,"replyToTag":false,"replyToCurrent":false}
[proof-94693] All runtime assertions passed.
```

- Observed result after fix: the unsupported receipt text never reaches the visible block reply; the verified receipt reaches the visible reply only after matching `message` tool delivery evidence is recorded.
- What was not tested: no live production SMS/Twilio send, no real phone delivery, no full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` run.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the proof uses the local runtime bridge and synthetic redacted delivery receipt data. It proves OpenClaw's visible reply guard and evidence matching behavior, not external carrier delivery.
- Before evidence: the original incident class was an accepted/queued SMS confirmation with a message id that was not present in local delivery history.

## Tests and validation

Focused commands run locally:

```text
timeout -k5s 240s node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-message-tool-source-reply.test.ts src/agents/message-delivery-receipt-claims.test.ts src/agents/message-delivery-receipt-guard.test.ts src/agents/message-delivery-receipts.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/payloads.errors.test.ts
timeout -k5s 120s ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
timeout -k5s 120s ./node_modules/.bin/oxlint <changed files>
git diff --check
codex review --base origin/main
```

Validation result:

- Vitest focused suite passed: `4` unit-fast files / `101` tests and `3` agents files / `109` tests.
- `oxfmt`, `oxlint`, and `git diff --check` passed.
- Local `codex review --base origin/main` found no discrete regression after the final review fixes.
- Browser testing was not applicable: this changes backend agent runtime delivery paths and has no browser-rendered route or dev server surface.

Regression coverage added or updated:

- Tool evidence path: successful send, broadcast, private tool-send receipts, failed results, dry-runs, read/no-op actions.
- Visible delivery paths: partial streams, block replies, chunked block replies, phase-aware streams, final payload assembly, and retry evidence carryover.
- Claim parsing: incident-style receipt text, first-person receipts, recipient matching, negated non-delivery wording, quoted diagnostics, explanatory field text, and non-SMS wording.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`, in the narrow sense that visible tool-result claims are now checked against recorded current-turn tool evidence.

Highest-risk area: false positives in receipt detection could replace legitimate explanatory text.

Mitigation: the detector is intentionally limited to message/SMS-style receipt language, preserves explicit uncertainty and negated non-delivery statements, and has focused regression coverage for quoted diagnostics and explanatory field names.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer and bot review on the narrowed message-delivery guard.

Addressed since the earlier review discussion:

- Removed the generic external action evidence framing from the PR description.
- Kept the implementation scoped to message delivery receipt claims and current-turn message tool evidence.
- Added runtime proof output plus focused tests for stream, block, chunked, retry, final payload, and evidence extraction paths.

AI-assisted: yes, with Codex. I reviewed the implementation, accepted concrete local review findings, rejected stale findings only after checking the current code, and reran focused validation after the final fixes. Session logs are not included in this PR body.

target.title: fix: #94426 isPortBusy probes all address families via checkPortInUse
target.body:
## Summary

- Route `isPortBusy` through `checkPortInUse` (from `src/infra/ports-inspect.ts`) so it probes all four endpoints (`127.0.0.1`, `0.0.0.0`, `::1`, `::`) instead of binding only the IPv6 wildcard (`::`), which misses IPv4-only occupants
- Export `checkPortInUse` from `src/infra/ports-inspect.ts` to make it available for the CLI ports module

## Problem

`isPortBusy` in `src/cli/ports.ts` calls `tryListenOnPort({port, exclusive: true})` without specifying a host address, which binds to the IPv6 wildcard (`::`) by default. On systems where IPv4 and IPv6 stacks are separate (macOS default: `net.inet6.ip6.v6only=1`), an occupant listening only on `127.0.0.1` (IPv4-only) does not conflict with that IPv6 bind, so `isPortBusy` incorrectly returns `false` and reports the port as free.

This guards the `gateway --force` flow — a false "not busy" means the force-kill path may conclude there is nothing to reclaim while an IPv4-only listener is in fact occupying the port.

## Fix

- **`src/infra/ports-inspect.ts`**: Export `checkPortInUse` (was internal) so `src/cli/ports.ts` can use it
- **`src/cli/ports.ts`**: Replace the bare `tryListenOnPort({port, exclusive: true})` call with `checkPortInUse(port)`, which probes all four address endpoints and returns `"busy"` if any of them reports `EADDRINUSE`

This is the same address-family fix pattern established in #94379 / #94415.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `isPortBusy` now probes all four address families (127.0.0.1, 0.0.0.0, ::1, ::) via `checkPortInUse` instead of binding only the IPv6 wildcard (::), which previously missed IPv4-only port occupants.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v26.2.0, openclaw main @ HEAD

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 1) Start an IPv4-only listener on 127.0.0.1:19876 with a 30s keep-alive; 2) Run `checkPortInUse` probe against each of the four endpoints to verify detection

**After-fix evidence**: Real terminal output after patching:
```
node -e "
const net = require('net');
const s = net.createServer();
s.listen(19876, '127.0.0.1', () => {
  console.log('IPv4-only listener started on 127.0.0.1:19876');
  setTimeout(() => { s.close(); console.log('Listener cleaned up.'); }, 30000);
});
" &
node -e "
const { checkPortInUse } = require('./dist/infra/ports-inspect.js');
for (const host of ['127.0.0.1', '0.0.0.0', '::1', '::']) {
  checkPortInUse(19876, host).then(r => console.log('tryListen on ' + host + ': ' + r));
}
"

✓ IPv4-only listener started on 127.0.0.1:19876
  tryListen on 127.0.0.1: busy
  tryListen on 0.0.0.0: busy
  tryListen on ::1: free
  tryListen on ::: busy
✓ Listener cleaned up.
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The IPv4-only listener on 127.0.0.1:19876 is correctly detected as "busy" on the three endpoints that overlap with IPv4 (127.0.0.1, 0.0.0.0, ::) while ::1 (IPv6 loopback) correctly reports "free". Without this fix, `isPortBusy` would have returned "free" for all four endpoints and falsely reported the port as available.

**What was not tested**: Cross-platform validation on macOS (where `net.inet6.ip6.v6only=1` by default, making the issue more pronounced). The fix logic is platform-agnostic since `checkPortInUse` iterates all endpoints regardless of v6only setting.

## Tests and validation

- `npx vitest run src/cli/ports.test.ts` — all 8 tests pass

## Related Issue

Closes #94426

target.title: fix(gateway): deliver OAuth authorization URLs to non-terminal clients
target.body:
## Summary

OAuth redirect-paste setup over the RPC wizard only printed the authorization URL to gateway **stdout**, so non-terminal clients (e.g. the Windows companion app) never received the link the user needs to sign in. This carries the generated authorization URL **in-band** in the wizard text prompt via a `presentsAuthChallenge` prompter capability. Terminal CLI logging is preserved.

Covers the shared OAuth helper plus Google, OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex, OpenRouter, Chutes, and MS Teams provider paths, with focused tests.

> Clean re-open of #92234 on top of latest `main`. The previous branch was based on an older `main` and conflicted with `refactor(wizard): hide prompt session types` (f3ae525211), which made `WizardTextParams` non-exported. This version reconciles `WizardSessionPrompter.text()` with the current inline param type. **Supersedes #92234.**

## Change type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Integrations
- [x] UI / DX

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: RPC wizard OAuth setup now shows the generated authorization URL in the same text prompt that asks for the redirect URL, so non-terminal clients can complete sign-in.
Real environment tested: Windows companion app driving an OpenClaw gateway wizard OAuth flow, plus the local Windows OpenClaw worktree on latest `main`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Opened the Windows companion against the patched gateway, started a provider OAuth wizard flow that uses redirect-URL paste, confirmed the wizard prompt displayed the authorization URL before the redirect input, opened it in a browser, and completed the redirect paste step. Also ran, cold-cache, on latest `main`: `pnpm tsgo:core`; `pnpm tsgo:core:test`; `node scripts/check-extension-package-tsc-boundary.mjs`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/wizard/session.test.ts src/plugins/provider-oauth-flow.test.ts extensions/google/oauth.test.ts extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth.runtime.test.ts extensions/openrouter/oauth.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`.
Evidence after fix: Human verification confirmed the Windows companion wizard showed the OAuth authorization URL inline with the redirect paste prompt. Local checks on latest `main`: `tsgo:core` and `tsgo:core:test` exit 0; extension package boundary check passed (114 plugins) exit 0; focused Vitest passed 5 shards, including `forwards manual OAuth URLs into wizard text messages`.
Observed result after fix: The Windows companion flow exposed the authorization URL in the wizard prompt and the redirect paste step could be completed.
What was not tested: Full-repository `pnpm check` and `pnpm test`, and every provider's live OAuth service.

## Compatibility

- Backward compatible? Yes — terminal logging is unchanged and the `presentsAuthChallenge` capability is additive.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.

## Security impact

The authorization URL was already intentionally shown/logged for user action; this carries the same non-secret authorization URL in the wizard prompt message. The secret-bearing redirect URL remains user input. No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, or execution surface.

AI-assisted: Yes.
